action: Rezone outdated O/R and GB districts
description: Phase 1: Complete analysis of obsolete Office/Research and General Business zones. Phase 2: Rezone to mixed-use Gateway or similar zoning enabling residential uses.
metric: Number of parcels rezoned; new housing units permitted in rezoned areas
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen; 2016 Housing Committee; HC Work Plan; 2017 Master Plan Action 1.2.1
notes: Section 10.686.30 of the Zoning Ordinance (as amended through Feb 17, 2026) establishes the Land Transfer Incentive Option within the GNOD. In lieu of building workforce units or paying in lieu, a developer may convey land to the City for below-market rate housing. Key mechanics: (1) conveyance requires City Council acceptance, not a Planning Board CUP; (2) minimum 1 contiguous acre of Developable Upland per 20 acres of GNOD development; (3) developer bears all costs including Phase I environmental, title, and closing; (4) Section 10.686.40 provides development rights banking when transfers exceed the minimum. City Council designates procurement, development form, and disposition of acquired parcels.
status_detail: Gateway Neighborhood Overlay District (GNOD) adopted 2024 for Commerce Way/Portsmouth Blvd parcels; allows up to 6 stories/120 units with workforce housing provisions
pros: Aligns zoning designations with current land use patterns and market demand.
Can generate new tax revenue from underperforming commercial parcels.
Unlocks housing on parcels where current permitted uses have limited market demand.
cons: May face opposition from existing commercial property owners concerned about property values
Requires careful transition provisions for existing nonconforming businesses
Infrastructure in these areas may not be sized for residential use without upgrades

description: Go beyond minimum state-mandated ADU changes: simplify design standards, allow detached ADUs in all residential zones, reduce dimensional barriers. Launch public education campaign, ADU Navigator program, and pre-approved plan library.
target: 50+ ADU permits within 3 years of adoption
metric: ADU permit applications per quarter; completed ADUs; affordability of ADU rents
sources: HAP Narrative; Feb 17 2026 Council; Keene 2A; South Portland 2D; HC Work Plan; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.1.1
notes:
Feb 17 second reading on state compliance amendments in progress. The pre-approved plan library component of this item is delivered through the Pre-approved residential design template program (new item), which expands scope to all small residential building types under a single expedited review track. This item retains all other components.

⚠ Compliance note (HB 577, eff. 7/1/25): Portsmouth's ordinance (amended through 5/5/25) still requires CUPs for most DADU configurations — unlawful under the new state law. The 30-day notice/sign-posting requirement for AP ADUs also requires legal review. Owner-occupancy provisions must be reviewed against HB 577 scope. Ordinance amendment is a legal obligation, not a policy choice.
status_detail: Planning Board reviewing amendments for HB 577 (eff. 7/1/25) and SB 284 (eff. 9/13/25); staff markup prepared Nov 2025; includes detached ADUs by right, 950 sq ft max
pros: Incremental density that is generally consistent with existing neighborhood scale.
Enables homeowners to generate income, helping them stay in place.
Uses existing infrastructure with minimal marginal cost to the city.
cons: Construction costs often exceed $150K, limiting who can actually build one
Neighborhood concerns about parking, character, and rental activity
Enforcement of owner-occupancy or affordability requirements is difficult

description: Permit and streamline commercial-to-residential conversions in Mixed Use and Gateway zones. Align with recent NH state law changes enabling office-to-housing conversions.
metric: Number of conversion applications and completed units
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen; HC Work Plan (Stebbins Thomas)
notes: ⚠ Compliance note (HB 631, eff. 7/1/26): State law mandates both multifamily on commercially zoned land and adaptive reuse conversion rights. Portsmouth's ordinance amendment must be in place before 7/1/26. See Z-25 and Z-29 for full compliance details.
status_detail: Council directed Planning Board (Sept 2025) to review Section 10.812 on conversion of pre-1980 dwellings; staff preparing amendments with workforce housing bonus concept
pros: Repurposes underutilized commercial space without new construction
Aligns with post-COVID office vacancy trends
Can add housing in walkable, already-serviced locations
cons: Building code upgrades (fire, egress, accessibility) can be expensive for converters
May reduce commercial tax base in some cases
Not all commercial buildings are structurally suitable for residential conversion

description: Phase 1: Comprehensive parking study for downtown/mixed-use zones. Phase 2: Implement parking maximums, shared parking, market-responsive standards. Consider separating on-site parking from development and creating developer contribution to municipal parking.
metric: Ratio of required-to-actual parking utilization; shared parking agreements
sources: HAP Narrative; Feb 17 2026 Council; Bill Bowen; South Portland 2C; Burien B1; 2017 Master Plan Actions 4.3.2, 4.3.3, 4.3.4
notes:
Feb 17 second reading on parking changes in progress

⚠ Compliance note (SB 284, eff. 9/13/25): Statewide cap of 1 parking space per residential unit is now in effect. Portsmouth's current ordinance requires 1.3 spaces for units over 750 sq ft — that requirement is unlawful. Parking reform for residential uses is a compliance obligation, not an elective policy reform.
status_detail: Two tracks: (a) SB 284 state mandate caps parking at 1/unit; (b) Council directed (Sept 2025) replacing parking CUP with site plan waiver; staff drafting revisions
pros: Reduces development cost — structured parking adds $30K-$60K per space
Frees land area for housing rather than surface lots
Aligned with state mandate (SB 284) capping parking at 1 per unit
cons: On-street parking pressure in established neighborhoods is a real concern
Residents with cars still need somewhere to park regardless of requirements
Political sensitivity — parking is often the loudest issue at public hearings

description: Allow duplexes, triplexes, fourplexes by-right in single-family zones with clear dimensional standards. Form-based standards ensure neighborhood compatibility. GIS analysis shows Portsmouth dedicates 85.8% of residential land (2,276 acres across R, SRA, SRB zones) exclusively to single-family homes while only 14.2% (375 acres in GRA/GRB/GRC) permits the multifamily types most residents can afford — a 6:1 disparity.
target: Ordinance adoption permitting 2-4 unit buildings in all residential zones
metric: Number of 2-4 unit permits issued; units produced by type
sources: HAP Narrative; South Portland 2A; Burien D1; 2016 Housing Committee; 2017 Listens; Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026); 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.2
notes:
DEPENDENCY: This item requires lot size reform (dimensional requirement updates) to proceed concurrently — missing middle allowances produce limited yield without companion dimensional changes that bring nonconforming parcels into compliance.

ANTI-SPECULATION PREREQUISITE: Ordinance drafting shall be preceded by a documented anti-speculation strategy endorsed by City Council. Land value capture is the preferred mechanism; equivalent approaches include land banking of target parcels ahead of upzoning, first-right-of-refusal provisions for nonprofit affordable developers on upzoned parcels, or transfer fees on short-hold resales of upzoned parcels. If a documented strategy is not endorsed by Council by the end of FY2027, the Planning Board shall report to Council on whether market conditions and parallel production targets justify proceeding without it. This prerequisite is a discipline on the reform, not a veto — the objective is to ensure that upzoning produces housing, not speculation.

Note: SB 282 (eff. 9/13/25) permits residential buildings up to 4 floors above grade with a single stairway, pending state building code rules from the BCRB. When those rules are set, this meaningfully reduces construction costs for 3–4 story missing middle infill and should be factored into feasibility modeling (see PT-11).
pros: Addresses the gap between single-family and large apartments that barely exists in Portsmouth
Historically consistent — many Portsmouth neighborhoods were originally built with duplexes and triplexes
Gentle density that can be designed to look like large single-family homes
GIS analysis shows multifamily-allowed zones currently generate 5.71x more assessed value per acre than single-family zones, suggesting potential fiscal benefits from increased density.
cons: Existing homeowners may oppose by-right allowances in SF zones
Land costs in Portsmouth may still make missing middle units expensive
Design standards need to be clear enough to prevent poor-quality projects

description: Offer increased density allowances for projects including affordable or workforce housing units. Define affordability thresholds (e.g., 80% AMI) and duration.
metric: Projects using density bonus; affordable units produced via bonus
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3C; South Portland; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.2
status_detail: GNOD includes density bonus (5 stories/80 units with public realm improvements; 6 stories/120 units with workforce housing, payment in lieu, or land transfer). Important distinction: land transfer path (Section 10.686.30) requires City Council approval rather than a Planning Board CUP — a structurally different approval track. Citywide density bonus expansion under consideration via HOP 2.0.
pros: Market-driven tool — developers voluntarily opt in when the bonus makes projects viable
Produces affordable units without direct public expenditure
GNOD provides working local precedent to build on
cons: If bonus isn't generous enough, developers won't use it (RKG found current structure underutilized)
Higher density may trigger neighborhood opposition even when voluntarily chosen
Requires ongoing monitoring to ensure affordable units remain affordable

description: Review and selectively increase building height limits in downtown, Gateway, and transit-proximate zones.
metric: Zones with updated height limits; additional capacity created
sources: HAP Narrative; 2017 Listens; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.2
pros: Even one additional story can significantly improve project economics
Targeted increases in appropriate locations don't affect residential neighborhoods
More height in fewer locations means less sprawl pressure on surrounding areas
cons: Strong community attachment to Portsmouth's existing skyline and character
Shadow, view, and wind impacts on adjacent properties
Risk of setting precedent that leads to broader height increases over time

action: Lot size and dimensional requirement updates
description: Reduce minimum lot sizes, frontage requirements, and setbacks in targeted zones for infill development. GIS analysis provides zone-specific data: R Zone currently requires 5-acre minimums (proposed: 1 acre), SRA requires 1-acre minimums (proposed: 15,000 sf), SRB requires 15,000 sf (proposed: 7,500 sf). These reductions would bring the majority of currently nonconforming parcels into compliance.
metric: Newly buildable parcels created by changes
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3B; Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026); 2017 Master Plan Action 2.1.3
pros: Brings thousands of legally nonconforming parcels into compliance, expanding options for homeowners seeking to modify or expand their properties.
Enables lot splits that create new housing opportunities using existing infrastructure — roads, utilities, and services already in place.
Reduces variance applications, saving homeowner cost and ZBA staff time while making housing evolution more predictable.
cons: Lot size reductions in established neighborhoods will face organized opposition from homeowners who value current density levels
Smaller lots may create stormwater, parking, and privacy concerns requiring updated design standards and performance requirements
Without accompanying infrastructure analysis, reduced lot sizes in areas with marginal sewer or water capacity could create service constraints

description: Create zoning for cottage housing clusters: small detached units around shared open space, reduced lot-per-unit requirements.
metric: Cottage housing projects permitted
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 4C
pros: Creates community-oriented housing with shared green space — appealing to seniors and small households
Lower per-unit costs than conventional construction
Gentle density model that tends to generate less neighborhood opposition
cons: Requires larger assembled parcels that are scarce in Portsmouth's built-out areas
Shared ownership and maintenance of common areas can create governance challenges
Limited developer experience with this model in New Hampshire

action: Inclusionary zoning with in-lieu fee option
description: Require or incentivize affordable units in developments above threshold size. Payment-in-lieu option funding Housing Trust Fund.
metric: Affordable units produced; in-lieu fees collected
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3C; South Portland
notes:
LEGAL NOTE: New Hampshire operates under Dillon's Rule principles. Incentive-based inclusionary zoning (voluntary density bonus in exchange for affordable units, as in GNOD) is legally defensible. Mandatory inclusionary zoning has not been clearly authorized statewide and could face challenge under RSA 674 and state preemption doctrine.

STATE ADVOCACY COMPONENT: HB 631 (eff. 7/1/26) made Portsmouth's inclusionary zoning more important and more legally exposed at the same time — by mandating multi-family residential on commercially zoned land, HB 631 will generate substantially more residential development on parcels where Portsmouth's IZ requirements apply, increasing both the stakes and the incentive to test the legal question. Portsmouth should support state legislation explicitly authorizing mandatory inclusionary zoning within safe-harbor parameters (affordability thresholds, project-size minimums, density bonus structure, in-lieu fee caps). This would protect existing GNOD workforce housing provisions from legal challenge and enable defensible citywide mandatory IZ adoption. Parameter calibration is critical: too-high requirements suppress production (see cons list); well-calibrated parameters produce units as a condition of market-rate development. Coordinate with PC-5 for state-level advocacy.
status_detail: RKG feasibility analysis completed Feb 2024; GNOD includes workforce housing requirement with 3 compliance paths (build, payment in lieu, land transfer); citywide IZ under discussion
pros: Produces affordable units as a condition of market-rate development
In-lieu fee option funds Housing Trust Fund for flexible deployment
GNOD provides working local model with three compliance paths
cons: If set too high, can discourage development altogether — calibration is critical
NH has no state enabling statute, creating potential legal vulnerability
Small project thresholds can disproportionately burden smaller developers

action: Form-based code reform for use deregulation
description: Reform Portsmouth's existing form-based code districts to deregulate use allowances as originally intended by the FBC framework.
metric: Pilot district adopted; development applications
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen
status_detail: Form-based code adopted in multiple Portsmouth districts (CD4, CD5, Gateway), but the original intent of deregulating uses was not carried through. Reform of existing FBC to address use allowances has not been initiated.
pros: FBC already adopted — reform is easier than starting from scratch
Deregulating uses while controlling form can enable housing diversity by right
Reduces CUP and variance burden on both applicants and boards
cons: FBC reform requires technical expertise and significant staff time
Risk of unintended consequences from use deregulation in historic areas
Some stakeholders may resist changes to a system they invested in creating

description: Create overlay zone(s) relaxing density, parking, and dimensional requirements for projects meeting affordability standards.
metric: Projects permitted under overlay; affordable units produced
sources: South Portland; 2016 Housing Committee; 2017 Master Plan Actions 3.2.1, 3.2.2
status_detail: Partially exists: GNOD includes workforce housing incentives; CD4, CD4W, and CD5 allow CUP for extra story in exchange for community space and workforce housing.
pros: Provides targeted incentives without changing underlying zoning citywide
Multiple existing overlays (GNOD, CD4/CD5 bonuses) provide foundation to build on
Can be designed to stack with other incentives for maximum impact
cons: Overlay complexity can confuse applicants and make the zoning code harder to navigate
Effectiveness depends on where overlays are mapped — wrong locations yield no projects
May concentrate affordable housing in specific areas rather than distributing citywide

action: Expand manufactured home and modular housing zoning
description: Phase 1: Review the Portsmouth Zoning Ordinance to establish the current baseline for manufactured and modular housing. Phase 2: Develop ordinance amendments to permit Cross-Mod and modular construction in appropriate residential districts beyond GA/MH.
target: Phase 1 review and staff readiness assessment completed within 9 months; Phase 2 ordinance amendments presented to Planning Board within 18 months
metric: Number of zoning provisions identified as barriers to off-site construction; Council action on ordinance amendments
sources: Bill Bowen; South Portland 2G; The Little Book of Low-Cost, High-Impact Housing Solutions (Ivory Innovations, October 2025), Chapter 6
notes: Manufactured homes are currently defined in the zoning ordinance only within the floodplain management section (Article 8). The term 'modular construction' does not appear in the adopted zoning ordinance as of May 5, 2025.
pros: Cross-Mod and modular housing offer quality comparable to site-built at 10–30% lower cost
Factory construction is faster and less weather-dependent than site-built
Closes a definitional gap that may be suppressing off-site construction without any deliberate policy decision
cons: Persistent stigma around manufactured housing despite significant quality improvements
Resale values for manufactured housing (HUD code) historically lag site-built homes
Zoning expansion will likely face neighborhood resistance and requires a managed engagement strategy

description: Reform live/work unit regulations to expand access and reduce restrictions. Key reforms: allow non-resident workers to be employed in live/work units (currently banned), and expand to south end districts.
metric: Live/work permits issued
sources: 2016 Master Plan (3.1.4); Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.1.4; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.1.4
notes: Much of the south end does not currently allow live-work units, despite the area's historic mixed-use character.
pros: Reduces commuting and supports small business / creative economy
Historically consistent with Portsmouth's south end mixed-use character
Allowing non-resident employees makes units more economically viable
cons: Parking and traffic impacts of commercial activity in residential areas
Code compliance complexity — units must meet both residential and commercial standards
Risk of units being used primarily as commercial space rather than housing

description: Rezone and plan new residential neighborhoods: Portsmouth Green/McKennon's area, Kane development (Commerce Way), southwest quadrant (Heritage Way, currently Industrial). Include infrastructure and recreation.
metric: Parcels rezoned; infrastructure plans adopted; units in pipeline
sources: Bill Bowen; 2017 Master Plan Action 1.2.2; Portsmouth Master Plan Update, Draft Framework (Utile/City of Portsmouth, March 2026)
notes: The draft Portsmouth Master Plan Update (Utile, March 2026) provides urban design frameworks for two of the three areas named above. Constitution Ave/Portsmouth Green (corresponds to Portsmouth Green/McKennon's): key strategies are to focus activity and development NW of Lafayette/Rt-1, establish a dual mixed-use core with NE-to-SW connectivity, connect to Community Campus and Greenway, and provide deliberate connections to lower-density residential uses to the SE. Outer Woodbury/Commerce Way (corresponds to Commerce Way/Kane): key strategies are to create cross-street connections unifying east and west sides of Woodbury Ave, calm traffic on Woodbury Ave, concentrate mixed-use near Woodbury Ave with multifamily set back, and strengthen Greenway connections. Note: the southwest quadrant (Heritage Way) is not identified as a Master Plan Neighborhood Center study area. Z-15's inclusion of that area reflects a broader planning ambition than the current Master Plan framework.
pros: Potential for significant unit counts on larger parcels
Can be designed as complete neighborhoods with amenities from the start
Reduces development pressure on established neighborhoods
cons: Greenfield development may conflict with conservation and open space goals
Requires substantial infrastructure investment (roads, utilities, schools)
Auto-dependent locations without transit access

description: Engage PDA on housing feasibility for GBCC students, National Guard, and Pease employees. A prerequisite first step is formalizing a liaison between the City Council and the Pease Development Authority (PDA) Board.
metric: Feasibility study completed; PDA engagement initiated
sources: Bill Bowen
notes: Requires PDA governance and federal deed restriction analysis. Federal BRAC deed covenants and FAA grant assurance restrictions may impose binding constraints on residential use at Pease.
pros: Large available land area with existing infrastructure
Could serve GBCC students, National Guard, and Pease workforce directly
Reduces commuting for thousands of Pease employees
cons: PDA governance structure may not prioritize or permit residential use
Environmental remediation concerns on former military base
Isolated from Portsmouth's walkable core — creates a separate enclave

action: Allow subdivision of existing larger houses
description: Amend zoning to permit subdivision of larger single-family homes into multiple units with appropriate restrictions.
metric: Subdivision permits; units created
sources: Bill Bowen; Keene 2A; Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026); 2017 Listens
notes: Many larger homes were historically multifamily and were subsequently converted to single-family use. Re-conversion to multifamily is one implementation pathway for this item.
pros: Many larger homes were historically multifamily — re-conversion restores original use
Adds units without new construction or changes to building footprint
Preserves neighborhood character while increasing housing supply
cons: Parking demand increases with more units in existing structures
Building code upgrades (fire separation, egress) add cost
Loss of larger single-family homes that serve families with children

description: Remove barriers to renting rooms in owner-occupied homes. Create home-sharing matching program (especially for seniors).
metric: Program enrollment; matches made
sources: Bill Bowen; Keene 2B
pros: Uses existing housing stock with zero construction cost
Particularly beneficial for seniors who want to age in place with rental income or companionship
Similar intent to ADUs but far less costly to implement
cons: Enforcement and quality control are difficult in informal arrangements
Landlord-tenant law complications for owner-occupied room rentals
Limited scale — helps individuals but doesn't significantly move supply numbers

description: Create zoning for seasonal worker dormitories or group housing for hospitality/tourism employment. Allow under conditional use permit or as of right in certain districts.
metric: Seasonal housing beds created
sources: Bill Bowen
notes: Implementation consideration: accommodating seasonal worker housing through CUP or by-right permissions within existing districts may be preferable to creating a new zone.
pros: Addresses a documented need in Portsmouth's hospitality and tourism sectors
Can be implemented through CUP or by-right in existing districts without new zoning
Supports businesses struggling with seasonal labor shortages
cons: Dormitory-style housing may face neighborhood opposition
Seasonal vacancy creates underutilized buildings for part of the year
Quality and safety standards for group housing need careful definition

description: Review and reduce minimum unit size requirements. Encourage smaller, more affordable units.
metric: Micro-unit permits issued
sources: 2016 Master Plan (3.2.5); South Portland 2G; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.2.5; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.5
notes: Currently allowed but few in pipeline. Further study needed to ensure micro-units actually get developed, not just permitted on paper.
pros: Lower per-unit cost makes smaller units more affordable to both build and rent
Matches demographics — 85% of projected demand is from 1-2 person households
Efficient use of land in high-cost locations
cons: Market viability is uncertain — further study needed to confirm developer interest
Very small units may not be livable long-term, leading to high turnover
Risk of being perceived as substandard housing rather than intentional design

description: Full audit of zoning ordinance identifying provisions that constrain housing production. Prioritize amendments. GIS analysis quantifies the compliance crisis: 69.9% of parcels in single-family zones violate current zoning requirements.
metric: Audit completed; constraining provisions identified and amended
sources: HAP Narrative; Progress Tracker; Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026); Housing Policy 2016-01 §VI.2 Regulatory Audit; Portsmouth Master Plan Update, Draft Framework (Utile/City of Portsmouth, March 2026)
notes: Audit scope should include: (1) SB 283 (eff. ~Nov 2025) — Portsmouth ordinance must exclude below-grade areas from FAR calculations; affects feasibility for infill projects. (2) HB 457 (eff. 9/13/25) — ordinance must not restrict occupants below 2 per bedroom or impose familial/relationship-based occupancy rules; audit for any such provisions. (3) SB 284 parking cap (eff. 9/13/25) — see Z-4. (4) HB 577 ADU compliance — see Z-2, Z-36. (5) District consolidation — the draft Portsmouth Master Plan Update (Utile, March 2026) recommends streamlining and consolidating Portsmouth's zoning districts to improve conceptual clarity and reduce redundancy. The audit should identify consolidation candidates and produce a proposed simplified district structure as a named deliverable, alongside the amendment prioritization list.
status_detail: HOP 2.0 grant ($65,321, Nov 2024) engaged RKG Associates, but the July 2025 scope was narrow — evaluating whether existing density bonuses are working, not a comprehensive zoning audit.
pros: Identifies provisions in current zoning that limit housing production in a single systematic review.
Creates a roadmap for prioritized amendments rather than piecemeal changes.
Provides evidence base that reduces political risk of individual reforms.
cons: Comprehensive audits are time-intensive — risk of delaying action on known barriers
HOP 2.0 / RKG scope was narrower than expected; ensuring breadth is challenging
Audit findings may overwhelm limited staff capacity to implement changes

description: Make diverse housing an explicit zoning purpose in targeted areas so it doesn't require variances or special exceptions.
metric: Reduced variance/special exception applications for housing
sources: 2017 Listens
pros: Makes housing diversity an explicit policy goal rather than an exception
Reduces variance and CUP burden for housing types that should be routine
Directly addresses the gap between FBC form standards and permitted uses
cons: Broad use deregulation may allow incompatible uses if not carefully scoped
Political challenge of explicitly stating diverse housing as a zoning purpose
Requires coordination with ongoing FBC reform to avoid contradictory standards

action: TOD overlay near transit stops and corridors
description: Explore transit-oriented development strategies for areas near transit stops and corridors. Portsmouth lacks a functional public transit system that would support traditional TODs. Reframe toward ensuring transit stops are co-located with areas of dense and affordable housing.
metric: TOD overlay adopted; units permitted within overlay; transit ridership
sources: South Portland 1C; Keene 3B; Burien C2; HC Work Plan (PtL Goal 4)
notes: Planning consideration: TOD feasibility in Portsmouth is constrained by existing transit infrastructure. Co-locating transit improvements with existing density may be a more viable approach than generating density around new transit nodes.
pros: Good long-term principle — co-locating housing and transit reduces car dependence
Can guide future transit planning toward housing-rich corridors
Positions Portsmouth for state or federal transit investment
cons: Portsmouth lacks the public transit infrastructure to support traditional TOD
Only viable location (C&J terminal) is at Pease where housing isn't allowed
Risk of adopting a planning framework that doesn't match local conditions

description: Periodically review enforcement of current STR regulations and assess whether any gaps exist in long-term housing supply protection.
metric: STR registrations; units returned to long-term market
sources: South Portland 2F; Burien D3; HC Work Plan
notes: Short-term rental regulation is largely in place in Portsmouth. Any further action would require reopening existing ordinance provisions.
status_detail: STRs already largely banned in Portsmouth. Colleague advises against reopening the topic.
pros: Monitoring provides data to evaluate whether current enforcement is achieving intended goals without requiring new regulatory action.
Low-cost activity relative to other housing preservation strategies.
Data on enforcement actions supports continued policy defense.
cons: Current enforcement may have gaps that allow de facto STR activity
Some property owners argue STR bans reduce property rights
Lost tourism revenue compared to communities that allow regulated STRs

description: Amend O/R zoning to allow residential as-of-right (not just conversion), potentially enabling new ground-up multifamily.
metric: New residential permits in former O/R zones
sources: HC Work Plan (Stebbins Thomas); Bill Bowen; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.2
notes: ⚠ Compliance note (HB 631, eff. 7/1/26): Municipalities must allow multifamily residential on commercially zoned land where adequate infrastructure exists. Portsmouth's ordinance currently prohibits multifamily in most commercial zones — amendment required before 7/1/26. NHMA guidance flags significant ambiguity in 'commercially zoned' and 'adequate infrastructure' — legal counsel review required. Note: SB 284 (eff. 9/13/25) caps residential parking at 1 space/unit but is separate from this mandate. See also Z-3 and Z-29.
status_detail: GNOD partially addresses for specific parcels; state mandate (eff. July 2026) will require multifamily by right in all commercial zones; implementation details still being legislated
pros: O/R zones have infrastructure and are often underutilized for their current permitted uses.
Voluntary adoption would align Portsmouth with anticipated state requirements before the effective date.
Enables ground-up multifamily rather than just conversions.
cons: May accelerate loss of commercial/office space that the city still values
Traffic and infrastructure impacts of adding residential to commercial corridors
Ground-up residential in O/R zones may not produce affordable units without requirements

action: Reduce conditional use permits for housing
description: Identify housing types currently requiring CUPs that could be allowed by-right with clear standards.
metric: Housing types moved from CUP to by-right; processing time reduction
sources: HC Work Plan (Kozak); Keene 2A
status_detail: Parking CUP replacement with site plan waiver is first step; RKG audit examining other CUP barriers
pros: Reduces time and cost for applicants and reduces workload for volunteer boards
Increases predictability for developers, encouraging more housing proposals
Parking CUP replacement with site plan waiver is a proven first step
cons: CUPs provide boards with discretionary review that some neighbors value
By-right development reduces community input on individual projects
Standards must be clear enough to replace case-by-case judgment

description: Study Industrial-zoned land (especially southwest quadrant) for housing suitability. Reclassify where industrial use is obsolete.
metric: Study completed; parcels reclassified
sources: HC Work Plan (PtL Goal 9); Bill Bowen (Heritage Way)
pros: Some industrial-zoned land (especially southwest quadrant) may be better suited to housing
Could unlock significant acreage for residential development
Reduces conflicts between residential and industrial neighbors
cons: Environmental contamination on former industrial sites may require costly remediation
Preserving industrial land protects blue-collar jobs and economic diversity
Study adds to planning staff workload without immediate housing production

description: Adopt zoning provisions requiring or incentivizing mixed-income developments across AMI bands (30%, 50%, 80%, 120%+).
metric: Mixed-income developments approved; unit distribution by AMI
sources: Keene 3C; South Portland; Burien A2; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.2.2
pros: Creates economically diverse communities rather than income-segregated developments
Projects serving multiple AMI bands can access more financing sources
Reduces stigma associated with concentrated affordable housing
cons: Complex to administer — monitoring multiple AMI tiers across developments
Development economics are harder to pencil with mixed-income requirements
Market-rate buyers may resist mixed-income communities, affecting sales velocity

description: Simplify permitting for conversion of historic or underutilized non-residential buildings to housing. Align with RSA 79-E.
metric: Adaptive reuse permits; units created from non-residential buildings
sources: Feb 17 2026 Council (RSA 79-E); Bill Bowen; 2016 Master Plan; 2017 Master Plan Action 1.1.1
notes: ⚠ Compliance note (HB 631, eff. 7/1/26): State law will require automatic exemptions from setbacks, height, and frontage for existing buildings converted to residential through adaptive reuse, provided the building footprint does not change. This is a compliance obligation effective 7/1/26. See also Z-3 and Z-25.
pros: Preserves historic buildings while adding housing — a double policy win
RSA 79-E provides existing tax incentive framework to leverage
Often faster and less costly than new construction
cons: Historic buildings may have structural limitations that constrain unit counts
HDC review adds time and uncertainty for adaptive reuse projects
Building code compliance in older structures can be expensive and unpredictable

description: Adopt or strengthen local workforce housing ordinance consistent with NH RSA 674:58-61.
metric: Workforce housing units approved under ordinance
sources: NH RSA 674:58-61; Keene; 2016 Master Plan
status_detail: Workforce housing provisions exist in GNOD but no standalone citywide workforce housing ordinance per RSA 674:58-61 framework
pros: RSA 674:58-61 provides clear state legal framework — reduces legal risk
Establishes a citywide baseline rather than relying on project-by-project negotiation
Creates affirmative obligation to provide reasonable opportunities for workforce housing
cons: NH workforce housing statute is primarily a defense against exclusionary zoning, not a production tool
May not produce units in practice without complementary incentives
GNOD already includes workforce provisions — citywide extension may face political resistance

description: Create zoning incentives for senior housing: relaxed parking, density bonuses, streamlined approval for age-restricted communities.
metric: Senior housing units permitted; age-restricted developments approved
sources: HC Work Plan (Loane); Keene 4C; RKG 2022 (growing 65+ population)
pros: Addresses fastest-growing demographic — seniors 65+ are a large and growing share of Portsmouth
Lower parking demand for senior housing makes it easier to build
Encourages turnover of single-family homes to younger families
cons: Age-restricted housing reduces flexibility — units can't serve other populations
Risk of oversupply in age-restricted category while other needs go unmet
Fair Housing Act limits on age restrictions require careful legal compliance

description: Create zoning incentives encouraging 2-3 bedroom unit production in new multifamily. RKG documents increasing family households and unmet demand for larger units while pipeline skews toward 1BR/studio.
target: Minimum 30% of new multifamily units to be 2+ bedrooms
metric: Share of new permits that are 2+ BR; family-sized units in pipeline
sources: RKG 2022 (household composition trends, survey)
pros: Market strongly underproduces 2-3 bedroom units — incentives address a documented gap
Retains and attracts families with children, supporting school enrollment and community vitality
RKG data shows increasing family households despite limited supply
cons: Larger units are more expensive to build, making affordability harder to achieve
Developers prefer studios and 1-bedrooms for higher per-square-foot returns
Incentives may need to be substantial to change developer behavior

action: Single-family zone reform strategy (R, SRA, SRB)
description: Comprehensive reform of Portsmouth's three single-family-only zones, which together represent 2,276 acres — 85.8% of all residential land.
target: Phase 1: Council workshop presentation of fiscal and compliance data (2026). Phase 2: Draft zone-specific amendments with public input. Phase 3: Planning Board review and Council adoption.
metric: Parcels brought into zoning compliance per zone; net new housing units permitted in reformed zones; tax revenue per acre trend; lot splits and two-family conversions per year.
notes:
SEQUENCING PREREQUISITE: Ordinance drafting for single-family zone reform shall be preceded by a documented anti-speculation strategy endorsed by City Council. Land value capture is the preferred mechanism; equivalent approaches (land banking, first-right-of-refusal for nonprofit affordable developers, short-hold transfer fees) satisfy this prerequisite if Council finds they address the speculation risk at scale. If no documented strategy is endorsed by end of FY2027, the Planning Board shall report to Council on whether to proceed, defer, or stage reform in zones where speculation risk is lowest (e.g., SRB staging in the draft Master Plan). The prerequisite reflects the HAP's recognition that upzoning without anti-speculation discipline captures value for existing owners rather than producing housing. It is a discipline on the reform, not a veto.

SRB STAGING (draft Master Plan): The draft Portsmouth Master Plan Update (Utile, March 2026) analyzed the SRB district specifically and recommends a two-stage approach: Stage 1 — reduce minimum lot size from 15,000 sf to 7,500 sf and allow two-family use by-right (see Z-8 and Z-5); Stage 2 — allow small multifamily by-right (see Z-5). Supporting data from the Master Plan: 1,701 lots across 1,362 acres, average lot size 9,317 sf, 64% of lots currently nonconforming by size. The Master Plan identifies SRB as the highest-priority zone for reform given the scale of nonconformance and the relative political manageability of staged implementation.
sources: Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026); NH State GIS database; 2017 Listens; 2017 Master Plan Action 2.1.3; Portsmouth Master Plan Update, Draft Framework (Utile/City of Portsmouth, March 2026)
pros: Addresses 85.8% of residential land, which is currently zoned exclusively for single-family development.
Brings thousands of legally nonconforming parcels into compliance.
Multifamily zones currently generate 5.71x more assessed value per acre ($5.9M vs. $1.0M).
cons: Single-family zone reform is the most politically volatile housing action — homeowner opposition will be intense and organized
Zone-specific proposals need significant refinement through public process
Risk of speculative land acquisition driving up prices before reforms produce actual housing

description: Developer exchanges banking of green space for site-specific density bonus.
sources: John Tabor/City of Dover
pros: Bypass zoning restrictions to get more density
CUP still required
cons: Limited by available conservation land
Perceived as spot zoning by neighborhoods

action: Revise co-living (Section 10.815) standards to reduce barriers to viable projects
description: Portsmouth adopted co-living as a permitted use in May 2025 (Ordinance #06-2025), establishing Section 10.815 of the Zoning Ordinance. Current restrictions limit co-living to four zones (GB, G1, G2, CD4-L1) by Conditional Use Permit.
notes: Recommended actions: 1) Evaluate adding co-living as a CUP use in MRB, OR, and WB districts 2) Evaluate reducing the parking standard 3) Evaluate permitting limited cooking facilities (kitchenette) 4) Evaluate increasing the per-building resident cap 5) Evaluate whether administrative approval is appropriate in G1/G2
sources: Progress Portsmouth
pros: Addresses a gap in housing options for residents for whom conventional rentals are financially out of reach
Co-living can achieve higher unit density per square foot than conventional multifamily
Kitchenette allowance may expand market viability without materially changing neighborhood character
cons: Expanding to MRB may draw opposition given its proximity to residential neighborhoods
Further parking reductions may face scrutiny in the Route 1 corridor absent transit alternatives
Kitchenette permission may complicate fire and building code compliance review

action: Monitor 2026 NH ADU legislation for conflicts with Portsmouth ordinance
description: Three bills in the 2026 NH legislative session have direct implications for Portsmouth's ADU ordinance adopted February 17, 2026. Planning Department should review each bill against the adopted ordinance on an ongoing basis.
metric: Planning Department review completed for each bill; formal city position taken before floor votes where conflict is identified
sources: Progress Portsmouth; NH General Court 2026 session
notes:
Bills to monitor: HB1079, HB1540, HB1662.

⚠ Compliance note: Review of the adopted ordinance (as amended through 5/5/25) against HB 577 (eff. 7/1/25) confirms non-compliance: CUP requirements remain for most DADU configurations; 30-day notice process for AP ADUs requires legal review; size limits and aesthetic standards need audit against new law's constraints. SB 284 (eff. 9/13/25) parking cap also requires ordinance amendment (see Z-4). HB 457 (eff. 9/13/25) prohibits occupancy restrictions based on familial/relationship status — Portsmouth ordinance should be audited for any such provisions (see Z-18, Z-35). SB 283 (eff. ~Nov 2025) requires below-grade areas excluded from FAR calculations — affects feasibility modeling (see Z-21, PT-11).
status_detail: Bills in House committee as of March 2026. No Planning Department ordinance conflict review on record yet.
pros: Proactive review prevents Portsmouth from being caught off-guard by state preemption
HB1662 loan guarantees would directly support ADU production goals
HB1079 non-conforming structure provision could unlock ADU potential in older neighborhoods
cons: Legislative outcomes uncertain; monitoring effort may not yield actionable conflict
HB1540 uniform standards could constrain locally tailored provisions
Staff bandwidth for bill tracking competes with implementation priorities

action: Housing target area designation with area-specific design standards
description: Formally designate housing target areas with area-specific design standards and infrastructure priorities. The draft Portsmouth Master Plan Update (Utile, March 2026) identifies five Gateway Neighborhood Center study areas that should serve as the spatial organizing framework for this designation, replacing the 2016 Housing Policy's broader four-focus-area geography: (1) Outer Woodbury/Commerce Way; (2) West End; (3) Route 1 Gateway/Lafayette North; (4) Elwyn Crossing; (5) Constitution Ave/Portsmouth Green. Each study area has a developed urban design framework including key assets, issues, and design strategies produced through the Master Plan process.
target: At least 2 housing target areas formally designated with adopted design standards within 18 months
metric: Number of areas designated; design standards adopted; housing units permitted within designated areas
sources: Housing Policy 2016-01 §III.3 Housing Target Areas; §IV Focus Areas; 2017 Master Plan Action 2.1.3; Portsmouth Master Plan Update, Draft Framework (Utile/City of Portsmouth, March 2026)
notes: Distinct from GIS site inventory and TOD overlay. Should be scoped into zoning modernization consultant work. The five Master Plan study areas should be explicitly included in the zoning modernization consultant scope as the priority target areas for design standards and zoning refinements.
pros: Provides spatial framework for directing infrastructure investment and zoning reform in a coordinated way.
Area-specific standards allow design context to vary.
Grounds the zoning modernization consultant scope in city-designated priorities.
cons: Designation process can become contentious if residents interpret it as a signal of unwanted density.
Design guideline development requires staff time and likely consultant support.
Risk of 'target area' framing implying other areas are excluded from housing investment.

action: Entry-level ownership housing feasibility and production strategy
description: Phase 1: Engage Portsmouth-area residential builders and housing economists to establish whether market-rate entry-level for-sale housing production is financially viable in Portsmouth's current land cost environment. Phase 2: If Phase 1 establishes a viable pathway, identify specific zones where small-lot single-family and attached townhouse development could be permitted by right.
target: Phase 1 feasibility engagement completed within 12 months
metric: Phase 1: documented price point threshold and conditions under which viable entry-level production is feasible
sources: AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026); Housing Supply Accelerator Playbook Strategies 5, 6, 7.1
notes: Portsmouth's current dimensional standards make small-lot ownership production structurally impossible in most residential zones under current rules. Phase 1 must establish whether land cost, not zoning alone, is the binding constraint before Phase 2 zoning work is initiated.
pros: Addresses the most acute affordability gap — ownership access for working households without subsidy eligibility
Market-rate production, if viable, requires no ongoing public subsidy
Feasibility-first framing is honest about land cost constraints
cons: Portsmouth's land costs may make entry-level market-rate production financially unviable regardless of zoning
Without resale restrictions, entry-level units will be sold upmarket over time
Phase 2 requires explicit coordination with items on lot size and SF zone reform

action: Streamline site plan review for housing
description: Reduce review timelines. Establish maximum review period targets. Expedited track for affordability projects.
target: Reduce average review time by 30%
metric: Average days application-to-approval; projects on expedited track
sources: HAP Narrative; South Portland; 2017 Listens; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.1
notes: The Permit review timeline audit and enforcement framework addresses the legal authority question for enforceable deadlines and establishes the baseline data this item requires to set defensible targets.
pros: Faster approvals reduce carrying costs for developers, potentially lowering housing costs
Expedited track for affordable projects aligns review resources with city priorities
Predictable timelines attract more developers to Portsmouth
cons: Faster review must not mean less thorough review — quality matters
Staff capacity may not support expedited timelines without process changes
Prioritizing some projects may delay others, creating equity concerns

description: Enable Planning Director approval with 30-day max review. Eliminate board hearings for compliant applications.
metric: ADU applications processed administratively; average approval time
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 2A
status_detail: Many ADUs already permitted by right under HB 577 (eff. 7/1/25). Only detached units and those with complexities require administrative approval.
pros: Most ADUs are already by-right under HB 577 — this formalizes existing practice
Eliminates board hearing costs and delays for straightforward applications
Frees board time for complex projects that genuinely need deliberation
cons: Removes neighbor notification and input for some projects
Administrative approval concentrates decision-making in one staff role
Complex cases still need clear criteria for when they escalate to board review

description: Improve digital permit system: status tracking, automated checks, online payment.
metric: Online applications as % of total; average processing time
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan
pros: Reduces in-person visits and paper processing for both applicants and staff
Status tracking reduces phone calls and inquiries about application progress
Improves data collection for housing production monitoring
cons: Upfront software and implementation costs
Staff training and transition period during adoption
Digital access barriers for some applicants

action: Interdepartmental review coordination and concurrent review implementation
description: Map the current review workflow for standard residential permit applications. Redesign the workflow to maximize concurrent departmental review where feasible. Establish baseline median review time by application type before implementation.
target: Current workflow map completed within 6 months; revised concurrent review workflow implemented within 12 months
metric: Number of review steps converted from sequential to concurrent; change in median review time
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan; Housing Policy 2016-01 §VI.4 Collaboration
notes: This item is administrative in nature and does not require Council action or ordinance amendment.
pros: Implementable through administrative policy, not ordinance — faster path to action
No additional cost; the mechanism is workflow redesign, not new staff or technology
Directly addresses a documented cause of permit delay
cons: Requires genuine coordination and sustained buy-in across multiple departments
Some sequential dependencies are real and technically necessary
Without the permit timeline baseline audit, the highest-leverage bottlenecks are unknown

description: Publish clear, objective design guidelines for the Character Districts and Gateway Neighborhoods Districts intended to substitute for open-ended discretionary design review for compliant applications in those districts. Provide applicant checklists. Objective standards that replace discretionary review reduce the unpredictability that deters infill investment and constrain the ability of board members to apply personal aesthetic preferences as approval criteria.
metric: Design guideline document published; reduction in revision cycles
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen; 2017 Master Plan Action 2.1.2; Portsmouth Master Plan Update, Draft Framework (Utile/City of Portsmouth, March 2026)
notes: The draft Portsmouth Master Plan Update (Utile, March 2026) specifically recommends publishing objective design guidelines for Gateway and Character district applications "in lieu of more open-ended discretionary design review" — treating this as a process reform, not a documentation effort. The distinction between supplementing and replacing discretionary review is intentional: supplementary guidelines leave board discretion intact and do not improve development predictability; substitutive guidelines define what gets approved and change applicant behavior upstream.
pros: Objective standards reduce subjectivity that leads to unpredictable board decisions
Clear checklists give applicants confidence about what will be approved
Reduces appeals and litigation from ambiguous review criteria
cons: Overly prescriptive guidelines can stifle architectural creativity
Developing clear guidelines is time-intensive and may require consultant support
Board members may resist constraints on their discretionary judgment

description: Formalize optional pre-application conferences for housing projects to identify issues before formal submission.
metric: Pre-application meetings held; application success rate
sources: South Portland; Keene
pros: Identifies deal-breakers before applicants invest in full design and engineering
Builds relationships between developers and staff, improving project outcomes
Reduces formal application revisions and resubmissions
cons: Informal guidance may be interpreted as commitments by applicants
Adds staff workload before any fee revenue is collected
Selective access to pre-application meetings could create fairness concerns

description: Review variance process for housing. Replace variance requirements with clear by-right standards where possible.
metric: Variance applications for housing; processing time
sources: HC Work Plan (Kozak)
pros: Replacing variances with clear by-right standards improves predictability.
Reduces ZBA caseload and meeting length.
High nonconformity rates may indicate that current zoning standards are not calibrated to existing development patterns.
cons: Variances exist for a reason — some projects genuinely need case-by-case review
By-right standards must be well-calibrated to avoid unintended development patterns
Reducing variances may be perceived as weakening neighborhood protections

description: Review all development-related fees. Reduce or waive fees for affordable/workforce projects.
metric: Fee schedule review completed; fee reductions for qualifying projects
sources: Keene 3D; HAP Narrative
pros: Fee reductions directly lower development costs, potentially passed through to tenants
Targeted waivers for affordable projects make those projects more financially viable
Demonstrates city commitment to housing production through concrete action
cons: Lost fee revenue must be absorbed by the general fund or other development
Fee waivers benefit developers — no guarantee savings reach end users
Determining eligibility thresholds adds administrative complexity

description: Regular training for planning, building, and public works staff on housing policy goals and streamlined processes.
metric: Training sessions conducted; staff participation
sources: Burien C1; HC Work Plan; Housing Policy 2016-01 §VI.3 Professional Development
pros: Ensures all city staff understand how their work connects to housing goals
Improves consistency of guidance across departments
Low cost relative to impact on application processing
cons: Training time competes with already-stretched staff capacity
Effectiveness depends on institutional follow-through, not just one-time sessions
May create tension if staff feel housing goals override other legitimate concerns

description: Identify or hire a person on city staff whose job is to understand state laws, financing, developer perspectives, and zoning for housing. Single point of contact.
metric: Position filled; projects assisted; time-to-permit reduction
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen; HC Work Plan; Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026)
notes: The Navigator function has been absorbed into Planning Department via staff reassignment rather than a dedicated hire. No specific staff lead has been named. Program-based items that depend on a human point of contact — ADU education, first-time homebuyer counseling, NOAH outreach — are at risk of becoming unowned unless a named staff lead is confirmed before those items receive launch timelines in triage. The planning capacity and workflow assessment (P-11) is now more critical: the same staff pool carries both application processing and Housing Action Plan implementation loads.
status_detail: The city will not hire a dedicated Housing Navigator. Planning Department will handle the coordination function through staff reassignment; no named lead has been identified as of March 2026.
pros: Single point of contact dramatically simplifies the process for applicants and developers
Person-to-person navigation is more effective than written guides or portals
HAP implementation needs a dedicated owner — without one, items languish
cons: Ongoing salary cost — previous state-grant-funded position wasn't sustained after grant ended
Scope risk — role can be overwhelmed if it becomes the catch-all for housing issues
Institutional knowledge is lost when navigator turns over

action: Planning department capacity and workflow assessment
description: Evaluate planning department workflows to identify process efficiencies that would free professional planning staff to engage in proactive policy development, housing strategy implementation, and long-range planning.
target: Assessment completed within first year of HAP adoption; efficiency measures implemented on rolling basis
metric: Ratio of staff time spent on application processing vs. proactive planning; number of HAP implementation tasks advanced per quarter
sources: Rick Chellman, Planning Board Chair; Progress Portsmouth (2026)
notes: Staff capacity is a key implementation variable: planning staff carry both application processing and HAP implementation loads. This assessment should identify where capacity constraints exist and recommend workload adjustments.
pros: Unlocks staff capacity for HAP implementation without new headcount — budget-compatible
Process reforms benefit all applicants, not just housing projects
Directly addresses the implementation bottleneck — without capacity, the HAP sits on a shelf
cons: Workflow assessment takes time that already-stretched staff may not have
Process changes face institutional inertia and staff resistance
Efficiency gains may be absorbed by growing application volume rather than freed for planning

action: Permit review timeline audit and enforcement framework
description: Phase 1: Conduct a baseline audit of current residential permit review timelines by application type. Simultaneously, commission a legal review of Portsmouth's authority to establish enforceable review deadlines. Phase 2: Based on findings, determine whether an enforceable shot clock mechanism is warranted.
target: Phase 1 audit and legal review completed within 12 months; Council direction on Phase 2 within 18 months
metric: Baseline permit review times by application type established in Phase 1
sources: AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026); Housing Supply Accelerator Playbook Strategies 2, 3, 4, 10
notes: Distinct from item P-1 (Streamline site plan review), which addresses process efficiency. This item addresses whether review timelines carry enforceable consequences.
pros: Grounds any future enforcement mechanism in documented data rather than assumption
Legal review protects Portsmouth from adopting an unenforceable ordinance
Phase 1 audit has independent value regardless of Phase 2 outcome
cons: NH legal authority for self-imposed shot clock consequences is uncertain
Enforceable deadlines require staff capacity to meet them consistently
Complex applications may not fit within fixed timelines without compromising review quality

action: Building code affordability and flexibility review
description: Phase 1: Establish Portsmouth's current building code baseline — specifically, whether the city applies the IRC or IBC to 2–6 unit residential buildings, and whether any local amendments add cost to housing construction without documented justification. Phase 2: Identify specific provisions for amendment.
target: Phase 1 baseline review completed within 9 months; Phase 2 recommendations to Planning Board and Council within 18 months
metric: Number of local code provisions identified as candidates for amendment; estimated per-unit construction cost impact
sources: AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026); Housing Supply Accelerator Playbook Strategy 11
notes: Review is explicitly limited to cost-impact analysis — this is not a proposal to reduce health, fire, or structural safety standards.
pros: Building code baseline is a factual question resolvable quickly at low cost
IRC application to small multifamily is already standard practice in peer NH cities
Single-staircase reform, if achievable, would materially expand Portsmouth's adaptive reuse pipeline
cons: Local code amendments require coordination with the NH State Fire Marshal and building officials
Some local amendments may have been adopted for documented local reasons
Single-staircase reform requires state action Portsmouth cannot compel

description: Establish a city-maintained library of pre-approved architectural designs for common residential building types including ADUs, duplexes, triplexes, small apartment buildings (up to 8 units), and cottage housing.
target: Library of at least 8 designs across at least 3 building types available, and expedited review track operational, within 24 months
metric: Number of templates available by building type; average permit review time for template applications vs. non-template applications
sources: AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026); Housing Supply Accelerator Playbook Strategies 4.1, 5.1, 10.3, 10.4
notes: This item subsumes the ADU pre-approved plan library referenced in item Z-2 (ADU ordinance enhancements) and expands its scope to all small residential building types.
pros: Reduces design and permitting cost for small-scale developers and homeowners
Standardizes review, reducing staff time and inconsistent outcomes across reviewers
Particularly beneficial for first-time developers and owner-builders
cons: Template library development requires upfront investment in architectural services or licensing fees
Maintaining and updating templates as codes change requires ongoing staff attention
May not serve developers with site-specific design requirements

action: Impact fee calibration by unit type and size
description: Analyze whether Portsmouth's development impact fees are accurately calibrated to the marginal infrastructure demand attributable to different housing types and unit sizes.
target: Calibration analysis completed as part of the next scheduled fee structure review cycle; findings presented to Council within 18 months
metric: Identification and quantification of any fee asymmetries by housing type; Council action on recommended adjustments
sources: AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026); Portsmouth Zoning Ordinance Article 14 (Impact Fees), Section 10.1430
notes: Structured as an expansion of item P-8 (Fee structure review), not a standalone item.
pros: Correctly calibrated fees reduce a barrier to small-unit and infill development without forgoing legitimate revenue
Provides a defensible, data-backed basis for any fee adjustments
Addresses the market broadly rather than only qualifying affordable projects
cons: Impact fee revenue funds capital infrastructure; any reduction must be reflected in the CIP
Analysis requires specialized expertise in infrastructure cost allocation
Fee adjustments require updated cost documentation before adoption

action: Third-party plan review authorization — legal assessment and pilot framework
description: Phase 1: Commission a legal review of Portsmouth's authority to authorize vetted private third-party plan reviewers to substitute for or supplement municipal staff review of residential building permit applications. Phase 2: If legal authority exists, develop a pilot framework.
target: Phase 1 legal review completed within 9 months; Phase 2 pilot framework developed within 18 months if legal authority is confirmed
metric: Legal authority determination documented in Phase 1; number of residential applications processed through the third-party track annually
sources: The Little Book of Low-Cost, High-Impact Housing Solutions (Ivory Innovations, October 2025), Chapter 1; AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026)
notes: Distinct from item P-12 (permit review timeline enforcement). This item addresses who conducts the review — a different lever that can reduce staff workload and cut timelines.
pros: Directly addresses staff capacity constraints without requiring new municipal hires
Third-party review has a documented track record across multiple states
Complements item P-12 without duplicating it
cons: NH legal authority to authorize third-party review is uncertain
Liability structure for inadequate third-party review requires careful legal drafting
Applicant cost pass-through for third-party reviewer fees may reduce appeal for smaller projects

description: 5-year property tax exemption for new ADU construction value. Affordability requirements for maximum benefit.
metric: ADU tax incentives granted; ADU rents vs. market
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 2A
notes: STATE CONTEXT (2026): HB 1662 (pending in 2026 session) would establish state-level ADU construction loan guarantees, directly addressing the financing barrier that a property tax exemption indirectly addresses. The mechanisms are complementary but not equivalent: loan guarantees reduce capital access barriers at construction; tax exemption reduces carrying costs after completion. If HB 1662 passes, I-1 should be evaluated for potential redundancy or reframed as a layered incentive specifically for affordability-restricted ADUs. If HB 1662 fails, I-1 moves up in priority as Portsmouth's primary response to the ADU financing gap. See also Z-36 for 2026 state bill tracking.
pros: Directly reduces the financial barrier to ADU construction for homeowners
Affordability requirements ensure public benefit in exchange for tax relief
5-year exemption window creates urgency to build
cons: Tax revenue foregone during exemption period — fiscal impact on city budget
May disproportionately benefit higher-income homeowners who can already afford construction
Exemption expires — no guarantee units remain affordable long-term

description: Waive building permit, impact, and planning review fees for projects with 25%+ affordable units.
metric: Fee waivers granted; affordable units produced with waivers
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3D
pros: Directly reduces development costs for affordable projects
Impact fee waivers are a proven tool used in many NH communities
Signals city prioritization of affordable housing
cons: Lost revenue from waivers must be made up elsewhere in the budget
Scope must be carefully calibrated — overly generous waivers create fiscal risk
Impact fees exist to cover real infrastructure costs that housing generates

description: Priority queue for projects targeting 80-120% AMI. 30-day turnaround guarantee.
metric: Workforce housing projects on expedited track; time savings
sources: HAP Narrative; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.2.1; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.1
pros: Time savings translate directly to lower carrying costs for workforce housing developers
30-day guarantee creates accountability for the review process
Targets 80-120% AMI — the workforce segment most likely to be financially viable
cons: Priority queue for some projects necessarily delays others
Staff capacity may not support guaranteed timelines without additional resources
Quality of review must not be sacrificed for speed

description: Expand RSA 79-E tax relief to more areas and project types. Promote existing program.
metric: RSA 79-E applications; investment leveraged
sources: Feb 17 2026 Council; 2016 Master Plan
status_detail: City Council work session held March 16, 2026. Portsmouth has not yet adopted a 79-E Housing Opportunity Zone. HB1103 (2026 session) passed House with amendment on March 3, 2026, expanding RSA 79-E credits.
pros: RSA 79-E is an existing, proven NH tool — no new legislation required
Tax relief encourages rehabilitation of aging housing stock
Conway's Housing Opportunity Zone (2024) provides NH precedent for expansion
cons: Foregone tax revenue during relief period
Benefits accrue to property owners who may not pass savings to tenants
Program awareness is low — underutilized even where currently available

description: Evaluate expanding Tax Increment Financing districts to support housing infrastructure.
metric: TIF-supported housing units; infrastructure investment
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3D
status_detail: Housing Committee Financial Tools working group discussed TIFs; no specific proposal advanced
pros: TIF captures increased tax revenue from new development to fund infrastructure
Self-financing mechanism — doesn't require general fund appropriation
Can make otherwise unfinanceable housing projects viable
cons: Diverts incremental tax revenue away from the general fund for the TIF period
Complex to administer and requires long-term financial management
No specific TIF proposal has been advanced despite Housing Committee discussion

description: Small grants for infill housing on underutilized parcels.
metric: Grants awarded; infill units produced
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3D
pros: Targets underutilized parcels — adds housing without sprawl
Small grants can be the tipping point for marginal projects
Demonstrates city investment in neighborhood-scale housing production
cons: Limited budget impact — small grants fund small projects
Identifying genuinely underutilized parcels requires GIS analysis and outreach
Grant administration costs may be disproportionate to housing produced

description: Proactively identify and utilize LIHTC, Historic Tax Credits, CDFI financing, NHHFA programs. Educate developers.
metric: Projects using tax credits; total funding leveraged
sources: 2017 Listens; Keene 1A
status_detail: PHA actively utilizing LIHTC (Sherburne 2026, Christ Church/Urban Forest CDFA credits); Ruth Griffin Place as successful precedent
pros: LIHTC and historic tax credits are the most powerful affordable housing production tools available
PHA has demonstrated track record
Educating developers expands the pipeline of competitive applications
cons: LIHTC is highly competitive — not all applications succeed
Tax credit projects have long compliance periods with complex ongoing requirements
City has limited control over state/federal allocation decisions

description: Expand property tax deferral or exemption programs for seniors on fixed incomes.
metric: Seniors enrolled; property tax relief provided; senior homeowner retention
sources: Burien A5; 2017 Listens
pros: Helps fixed-income seniors stay in their homes as property values rise
Reduces displacement pressure on long-term residents
Deferred taxes are eventually collected — it's timing relief, not forgiveness
cons: Deferred revenue creates cash flow gaps for the city
Liens on properties can complicate estate settlement for heirs
Benefits individual homeowners but doesn't produce new housing supply

description: Research feasibility of multifamily property tax exemption program. New Hampshire does not currently have enabling legislation for municipal multifamily tax exemptions — state legislative authorization would be required.
metric: Feasibility study completed; legislation pursued if viable
sources: Burien A1; South Portland
notes: May require NH legislative action
pros: Tax exemptions can significantly improve project feasibility for multifamily developers
Widely used in other states (Washington, Oregon) with documented impact
Can be tied to affordability requirements for public benefit
cons: NH may not have enabling legislation for municipal multifamily tax exemptions
Significant revenue impact if applied broadly
Research and feasibility study required before any implementation

description: Develop model for employer-funded housing assistance: down payment assistance, rental subsidies, employer-built housing.
metric: Employers participating; employee housing assistance provided
sources: Bill Bowen (Service Credit Union, Novocure, Lonza); Keene 1C; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.2.6
pros: Leverages private sector resources for a shared problem — housing affects recruitment
Models exist: hospital-provided housing, employer-assisted down payments
Shifts some housing cost burden from public sector to benefiting employers
cons: Voluntary programs have historically low participation rates
Smaller employers may not have resources to contribute
May primarily benefit higher-wage employees rather than those most housing-burdened

description: Provide startup funding, technical assistance, or land for a Portsmouth Community Land Trust.
metric: CLT established; permanently affordable units in CLT portfolio
sources: HAP Narrative; South Portland; Keene 1A
status_detail: Retired — superseded by the new item 'Shared equity and permanently affordable homeownership — partner identification and policy framework', which expands this item's scope with a phased structure, explicit partner engagement process, and a full policy framework.
pros: Permanently removes land cost from housing — units stay affordable through resale
Community ownership model builds local control and stewardship
Proven model in NH (Lakes Region CLT) and nationwide
cons: Significant startup costs and organizational capacity needed
Homeowners build equity more slowly than in fee-simple ownership
Requires ongoing organizational infrastructure and governance

description: Expand down payment assistance, closing cost support, and financial literacy programs.
metric: First-time buyers assisted; average assistance amount; homeownership rate
sources: Keene 1B; 2017 Listens; HC Work Plan; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.2.4; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.4
pros: Directly addresses affordability barrier for younger households being priced out
Financial literacy component builds long-term homeowner success
Expands access to wealth-building through homeownership
cons: Demand-side subsidies can push prices higher in a supply-constrained market
Benefits are per-household — limited impact on overall affordability
Student debt burden (40% of RKG survey respondents) may make buyers ineligible anyway

description: Develop or expand emergency rental assistance, security deposit assistance, and tenant legal aid.
metric: Renters assisted; evictions prevented; funds distributed
sources: Keene 1B; HC Work Plan; Burien A4
pros: Prevents homelessness — emergency assistance is far cheaper than shelter or rehousing
Security deposit assistance removes a major barrier to housing access
Can be funded through Housing Trust Fund
cons: Ongoing program costs with no asset created
Treats symptoms of unaffordability rather than root causes
Eligibility verification and administration require dedicated staff

description: Low-interest loans or grants for owner-occupants and small landlords to rehabilitate older housing stock.
metric: Rehab loans/grants awarded; units improved; code violations resolved
sources: Keene 2A; South Portland 1A; 2017 Listens; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.1.2; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.1.2
pros: Preserves existing housing stock — often cheaper than new construction per unit
Helps small landlords maintain properties without raising rents
Addresses health and safety issues in aging buildings
cons: Loan repayment can be uncertain — risk of default by small property owners
Benefits individual properties rather than systemic improvement
Program administration and property inspection costs

description: Actively promote existing employer-assisted housing programs. Expand eligibility and outreach.
metric: Program participants; employer partners; units accessed
sources: HC Work Plan (Migirditch); 2016 Master Plan
pros: Program already exists — requires marketing, not new program design
Employer-assisted housing programs attract and retain workers
Low cost to city relative to potential impact
cons: Awareness campaigns don't guarantee participation
Program may not be structured to serve lowest-income workers
Effectiveness depends on employer willingness to participate

description: Pursue state legislative authorization for dedicated local option revenue streams for municipal housing trust funds. Primary vehicle: local option real estate transfer fee — voter-approved, capped at a modest percentage of sale price (e.g., 0.5%), dedicated to the Housing Trust Fund, modeled on Vermont's Housing for All surcharge and Massachusetts's Community Preservation Act. Secondary vehicle: local option rooms and meals supplement (historically referred to as 'pillow tax'). Both require state enabling legislation; neither is currently available to New Hampshire municipalities.
metric: Legislation filed; revenue generated if enacted
sources: HC Work Plan (Kozak)
notes: Connects directly to HTF-2 and HTF-3. The Housing Trust Fund is currently capitalized at approximately $500K (per HTF-2 status detail). At current capitalization, the fund cannot finance meaningful production — it functions as a symbolic commitment. Vermont and Massachusetts have both used municipal-option transfer fees to scale local housing trust funds from symbolic to production-capable. Coordinate with PC-5 for state-level advocacy.
pros: Creates dedicated, sustainable revenue stream for Housing Trust Fund
Voter-approved at municipal level — builds local buy-in rather than imposed top-down
Vermont and Massachusetts precedents demonstrate the mechanism works at scale
Transfer fee is paid once at transaction, not recurring — lower political friction than property tax increases
Revenue scales with market activity, so high-transaction years produce more funding when land prices are most pressuring affordability
cons: Requires state legislative authorization — outside city control
New Hampshire Association of Realtors has historically opposed transfer fee legislation
Voter approval at municipal level adds a step that may delay deployment
Revenue fluctuates with real estate market cycles; produces less funding in downturns when need may be higher
Hospitality industry will likely oppose pillow tax variant

description: Additional density bonuses for projects incorporating universal design and ADA-accessible units beyond minimum requirements.
metric: Accessible units produced via bonus; % of new units meeting accessibility
sources: HC Work Plan (Loane); Keene 4C; South Portland
pros: Incentivizes universal design that benefits aging residents and people with disabilities
Density bonus is market-driven — developers opt in when it makes financial sense
Addresses a documented need in Portsmouth's aging housing stock
cons: Accessible units cost more to build — density bonus must offset the cost differential
May not produce enough accessible units to meet actual demand
