Requires clear standards for what qualifies beyond ADA minimums

description: Incentives combining historic preservation with housing production: streamlined historic review, tax credit stacking.
metric: Historic buildings converted to housing; incentives utilized
sources: 2016 Master Plan; RSA 79-E; South Portland
pros: Stacking historic and housing tax credits can make difficult projects viable
Preserves Portsmouth's architectural heritage while adding housing supply
Strong alignment with community values around historic character
cons: HDC review process adds time and uncertainty for developers
Historic preservation standards can conflict with housing efficiency goals
Tax credit stacking is complex and requires specialized development expertise

description: Dedicated incentive program (grants, low-interest loans, tax relief) for accessibility retrofits in pre-1960 housing stock.
metric: Retrofit incentives awarded; units made accessible; senior displacement prevented
sources: RKG 2022 (housing stock age, survey: accessibility needs); HC Work Plan (Loane)
pros: 73% of owner-occupied housing is pre-1960 — retrofits address a massive existing need
Enables aging in place, reducing demand on assisted living and nursing facilities
Directly serves seniors and people with disabilities
cons: Retrofit costs are high — grants/loans may cover only a fraction of need
Benefits individual homeowners rather than expanding overall supply
Program scale needed to make meaningful impact requires significant funding

action: Land value capture and anti-speculation strategy
description: Develop a coordinated strategy to capture land value increases resulting from upzoning and public investment, and to prevent speculative land price escalation. Tools include strategic land banking, resale and affordability covenants, enhanced TIF districts, and monitoring of land transaction prices in areas where zoning changes are proposed.
target: Strategy framework developed within first year of HAP adoption. Land price monitoring initiated immediately upon adoption of any major upzoning.
metric: Land transaction price trends in upzoned areas vs. citywide baseline; number of parcels acquired for housing through land banking; TIF revenue captured
sources: Reviewer feedback (2026); Progress Portsmouth (2026)
notes: SEQUENCING PREREQUISITE: This strategy must be in active development before missing middle housing allowances and single-family zone reform proceed to ordinance drafting.
pros: Prevents upzoning from primarily benefiting land speculators rather than producing housing
Land banking acquires parcels at pre-upzoning prices, dramatically reducing future housing development costs
Creates a self-reinforcing cycle: public investment increases land values, captured value funds more housing investment
cons: Land banking requires timely deployment of capital ahead of upzoning announcements
Land value capture mechanisms may face legal challenges under NH property rights framework
$500k fund capitalization is meaningful but limited relative to Portsmouth land values

action: Home Town first-time homebuyer program — feasibility audit and structural review
description: Conduct a structured audit of Portsmouth's Home Town program to assess whether its current design is functionally accessible in today's market. Several structural elements may render it inaccessible: FHA purchase price ceiling, insufficient assistance levels, $10,000 post-closing liquid asset cap, FHA condo approval requirements excluding 81% of new production, single-bank dependency.
target: Audit completed within first year of HAP adoption; modifications implemented within 18 months
metric: Annual program utilization (applications, approvals, closings); gap between maximum assistance level and median Portsmouth home price
sources: Home Town Program Guidelines Rev. 5/2025; Community Development Department; Housing Policy 2016-01 §III Master Plan Strategy 3.2.4
notes: Distinct from item I-14 (program expansion) and item I-17 (program promotion). Promotion without structural reform is counterproductive if the program is structurally inaccessible.
pros: Addresses a documented gap: the program exists on paper but may not be functionally reaching eligible households
Audit-first framing avoids promoting a program that may be structurally broken
FHA ceiling, asset cap, and single-bank structure are each independently fixable with modest administrative changes
cons: Audit findings may reveal the program requires significant restructuring or additional funding
Renegotiating the Citizens Bank partnership may face institutional resistance
If utilization data is very low, the finding could embarrass the city rather than prompt productive reform

action: Shared equity and permanently affordable homeownership — partner identification and policy framework
description: This item replaces and substantially expands item I-13 (Community Land Trust support). Phase 1: Engage potential operating partners — beginning with NH Community Loan Fund, Home For All, and Workforce Housing Coalition — to assess whether a shared equity homeownership program is viable. Phase 2: If a willing and capable partner is identified, develop a policy framework.
target: Phase 1 partner engagement completed within 12 months
metric: Phase 1: partner organizations assessed and recommendation to Council on program viability; Phase 2 if applicable: number of permanently affordable ownership units in pipeline within 36 months
sources: Housing Supply Accelerator Playbook Strategy 12.1
notes:
This item replaces item I-13 (Community Land Trust support), which has been retired. Portsmouth does not have the staff capacity to operate a CLT directly.

STATE ADVOCACY COMPONENT: Two state-level changes would materially improve shared equity homeownership feasibility in New Hampshire, regardless of which Phase 1 partner is identified: (1) RSA amendment clarifying that deed-restricted shared equity homes are assessed for property tax at their restricted resale value, not hypothetical unrestricted market value — current ambiguity inflates carrying costs and undermines the affordability math; (2) NHHFA Qualified Allocation Plan priority adjustment for shared equity homeownership projects to compete effectively with rental production for LIHTC allocations. Both items should be coordinated through PC-5. The property tax assessment clarification is the higher-leverage ask because it is a single RSA amendment with no fiscal note for the state and substantial impact on program viability.
pros: One-time public subsidy recycles through multiple households over time — significantly more efficient than ongoing rental subsidy
Addresses a genuine gap in the HAP — most current items target rental production
Regional approach through an existing partner organization avoids building organizational capacity from scratch
cons: No CLT organization currently operates on the Seacoast; Phase 1 may find no willing partner
Shared equity resale restrictions reduce individual wealth accumulation relative to unrestricted homeownership
CDBG and HOME funding is competitive and limited

action: Housing Trust Fund: establish governance and criteria
description: Establish clear deployment criteria. Create Housing Trust Fund Committee. Define eligible uses: gap financing, acquisition, predevelopment.
metric: Governance structure adopted; deployment criteria published
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 1A; South Portland
notes: Portsmouth has existing trust fund needing activation
status_detail: Below Market Rate Housing Trust Fund established by City Council (2025); governance crafted by Housing Committee, Legal, and Trustees
pros: Fund is already established with $500K seed — governance ensures effective deployment
Clear criteria prevent ad hoc spending and build public confidence
Committee oversight provides expertise and accountability
cons: Governance structure adds administrative overhead to fund operations
Committee composition and selection process can become political
Overly rigid criteria may limit fund flexibility to respond to opportunities

action: Housing Trust Fund: identify and secure funding sources
description: Identify sustainable streams: inclusionary in-lieu fees, ARPA, developer contributions, employer contributions, state grants.
target: Annual funding target: $___
metric: Annual contributions by source; fund balance
sources: Keene 1A; South Portland Rec. 3
status_detail: Fund seeded with $250K city + $250K SoBow Square LLC; accepts donations and grants
pros: Diverse funding streams reduce dependence on any single source
In-lieu fees from inclusionary zoning create a market-driven revenue source
State grants and employer contributions can multiply city investment
cons: Most funding sources are uncertain or competitive
Developer contributions may be resisted as an added cost of doing business
Fund balance may remain too small to finance meaningful projects without major sources

action: Housing Trust Fund: deploy for gap financing
description: Revolving loans/grants for affordable and workforce projects. Prioritize projects leveraging other sources.
metric: Loans/grants made; units produced; leverage ratio
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3D
pros: Gap financing is often the critical missing piece that makes affordable projects viable
Revolving loans recycle dollars — repayments fund future projects
Leverages other funding sources at a high multiplier
cons: Fund balance may be insufficient for meaningful gap financing at current levels
Loan underwriting requires financial expertise on staff or committee
Revolving loan repayment assumes projects generate enough revenue to repay

description: Comprehensive inventory of city-owned and state-owned land and underutilized facilities. Assess housing suitability.
metric: Inventory completed; parcels identified as housing-suitable
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen; Keene 3A; Burien B4
notes: Inventory should account for two distinct land pipelines: (1) city-owned and state-owned parcels (traditional scope); and (2) privately-owned GNOD parcels where the land transfer mechanism (Section 10.686.30) may bring land into public control. GNOD transfers, once accepted by City Council, are designated primarily for below-market rate housing. Tracking potential GNOD transfer sites alongside city-owned parcels gives the Housing Committee a more complete picture of the land available for affordable housing development.
pros: Comprehensive inventory is a one-time effort that informs multiple future decisions
Public land eliminates the largest cost component in housing development
Identifies opportunities that may not be apparent without systematic review
cons: Not all publicly owned land is suitable or desirable for housing
Land disposition is politically sensitive — competing interests for public parcels
Inventory alone doesn't produce housing — it's a planning step, not an action

action: Public land RFPs for housing development
description: Issue RFPs on suitable city-owned parcels. Require affordability commitments.
metric: RFPs issued; proposals received; units in pipeline from public land
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3A; South Portland
notes: GNOD land transfer parcels (Section 10.686.30) accepted by City Council should be evaluated for RFP issuance following the Sherburne ground lease model. City Council designates procurement and disposition method for each accepted parcel — RFP is one available mechanism. Coordinating GNOD transfer acceptance decisions with RFP readiness (Phase I clearance, constraints site plan) would reduce time between acquisition and developer selection.
status_detail: Sherburne RFP completed; Housing Committee discussed next sites including Gosling Meadows expansion; Master Plan expected to inform future identification
pros: RFP process ensures competitive selection of best development proposals
City can impose affordability and design requirements as conditions of land transfer
Sherburne RFP provides a successful local precedent
cons: City forgoes sale revenue if land is conveyed below market value for housing
RFP process takes time — from issuance to developer selection to construction
Limited inventory of suitable public parcels in a built-out city

description: Expedite approvals for PHA's 127-unit project. Coordinate city infrastructure improvements.
metric: Permits issued; infrastructure completed; units delivered
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan
status_detail: PHA selected as developer (Dec 2024, 8-1 Council vote). Phase I (90 units in new building) permitted; awarded 2026 LIHTC credits. Phase II (37 units including school conversion) timing and design not yet established.
pros: 127 units of affordable housing is significant for a city of Portsmouth's size
PHA as developer brings experience, mission alignment, and LIHTC track record
Phase I already permitted with 2026 LIHTC credits — momentum is real
cons: Phase II (37 units, school conversion) timing and design still unresolved
Project concentrates affordable units in one location
City infrastructure improvements needed to support the development

description: Work with PHA and housing nonprofits to identify next large-scale affordable housing site.
metric: Site identified; feasibility study completed; development timeline
sources: Bill Bowen; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.7
notes: GNOD land transfer mechanism (Section 10.686.30) represents a pathway for large-scale site creation without city expenditure. As GNOD development applications come forward, Housing Committee should track whether land transfer option is being used and whether transferred parcels could serve as next large-scale affordable site. Complements PL-1 and PL-2 in building a forward land pipeline.
pros: Pipeline planning ensures continuity of affordable housing production after Sherburne
PHA's track record reduces risk compared to unknown developers
Early site identification allows time for community engagement and planning
cons: Suitable large-scale sites are increasingly scarce in Portsmouth
Large affordable housing projects can face concentrated neighborhood opposition
PHA capacity is finite — multiple simultaneous projects may stretch resources

description: Evaluate municipal buildings being vacated or underutilized for housing conversion.
metric: Buildings assessed; conversion feasibility determined
sources: Bill Bowen; Keene 3A
pros: Adaptive reuse of municipal buildings avoids demolition and new construction costs
Public ownership eliminates land acquisition cost
Demonstrates city leadership by using its own resources for housing
cons: Few municipal buildings are actually being vacated or are suitable for housing
Conversion costs for institutional buildings can be high
Community attachment to existing municipal buildings may create opposition

description: Coordinate with School Board to assess surplus school properties for housing potential.
metric: School properties assessed; housing feasibility determined
sources: HC Work Plan (Corsetti)
pros: School Board coordination ensures housing planning aligns with enrollment trends
Surplus school properties are often well-located in neighborhoods
Sherburne School provides a direct precedent for school-to-housing conversion
cons: School Board and City Council are separate governing bodies with different priorities
Communities often have strong emotional attachment to school buildings
'Surplus' determination can be controversial when enrollment projections are uncertain

action: Religious and institutional land inventory and housing partnership program
description: Phase 1: Conduct an inventory of land parcels held by religious organizations, nonprofits, and educational institutions citywide. Phase 2: Reach out to willing landowners to discuss housing development interest.
target: Phase 1 inventory and zoning review completed within 12 months
metric: Number of institutional parcels inventoried; number of willing partner organizations identified; number of units in active development pipeline on institutional land within 36 months
sources: AEI State Housing Supply Legislation Update (March 2026); Housing Supply Accelerator Playbook Strategies 5.5, 10.4, 11.3
notes: Complements item PL-1 (Public land inventory for housing), which covers city-owned and state-owned parcels only.
pros: Unlocks potential development sites in walkable, service-accessible locations
Faith-based and nonprofit organizations are frequently mission-aligned with affordable and workforce housing goals
City role is facilitation and barrier removal — no public land acquisition cost
cons: Entirely dependent on willingness of individual institutions; no mechanism to compel participation
Complex ownership and governance structures may complicate long-term affordability covenants
Inventory may reveal fewer viable sites than expected

description: Convene major employers (hospital, schools, hotels, Service Credit Union, Novocure, Lonza) on workforce housing.
metric: Employer roundtable convened; partnerships formed; employer-assisted units
sources: HAP Narrative; Bill Bowen; Keene 1C
pros: Builds coalition of stakeholders who can advocate for housing beyond government
Employers can contribute resources, land, and political support
Reframes housing as an economic competitiveness issue, not just a social concern
cons: Employer engagement often stalls after initial meetings without concrete asks
Competitive dynamics between employers may limit candid information sharing
Risk of corporate interests dominating the housing agenda

description: Recruit and support nonprofit housing developers (NeighborWorks, CATCH, local CDCs).
metric: Nonprofit developers engaged; projects in pipeline
sources: Bill Bowen; HC Work Plan (Migirditch; Snyder)
status_detail: PHA is lead partner on Sherburne and Urban Forest/Christ Church projects; POAH was Sherburne finalist; Housing Committee discussing broader nonprofit engagement
pros: Mission-driven developers prioritize long-term affordability over profit
Nonprofits access funding sources unavailable to for-profit developers
PHA and POAH involvement in Sherburne demonstrates existing capacity
cons: Nonprofit developer capacity is limited — few organizations serve NH seacoast
Nonprofit projects typically take longer to develop than for-profit
Reliance on nonprofits may reduce city's push for private-market housing reform

description: Form or join regional coalition with Dover, Rochester, Exeter, Kittery for shared strategies.
metric: Coalition formed; regional projects initiated
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 1A; Burien C3
pros: Housing markets are regional — solutions require cross-municipal coordination
Shared advocacy increases political weight with state legislature
Cost-sharing on research, data, and consulting reduces per-community burden
cons: Regional coalitions are slow to form and difficult to sustain
Different communities have different housing needs, incomes, and politics
Risk that Portsmouth's specific urgency gets diluted in regional framework

description: Conduct listening sessions to understand development barriers. Invite for-profit and nonprofit developers.
metric: Sessions held; barriers identified; projects resulting
sources: HC Work Plan (Migirditch; Snyder)
pros: Developers know firsthand what barriers prevent housing production in Portsmouth
Listening sessions are low-cost and can yield immediately actionable insights
Demonstrates city openness to working with the development community
cons: Developer feedback will naturally favor reducing regulations and requirements
Sessions may raise expectations that all identified barriers will be removed
Public perception risk if seen as city catering to developer interests

description: Maintain an active, prioritized list of state legislative asks connected to HAP items that require state enabling. Brief state legislators representing Portsmouth (Senate: Kwoka; House delegation) at least twice per legislative session. Coordinate with Workforce Housing Coalition, NH Community Loan Fund, NH Housing, and municipal peers.
metric: Legislative contacts; bills filed/supported; legislation enacted
sources: HC Work Plan (PtL Goal 5; Kozak)
notes:
CURRENT PRIORITY STATE-LEVEL ASKS (2026–2027 sessions):
1. Inclusionary zoning safe-harbor authorization (Z-10)
2. Local option real estate transfer fee for municipal housing trust funds (I-16)
3. Shared equity homeownership property tax assessment clarification (I-22)
4. Support HB 1662 (ADU loan guarantees) and HB 1079 (non-conforming structure ADU flexibility); monitor HB 1540 (Z-36, I-1)
5. Parking benefit district enabling legislation (Z-4, IS-2)
6. Research: land value capture enabling legislation (Z-5, Z-33) — medium-term

This list updates at minimum annually based on HAP progress, state legislative landscape, and identified gaps. It is Portsmouth's institutional memory for state-level work that municipal action alone cannot resolve.
status_detail: City actively tracked and responded to HB 577 and SB 284; Planning staff coordinating with NHMA on 2026 legislative session
pros: State-level changes (HB 577, SB 284) have been the most impactful housing reforms
Coordinated advocacy through NHMA multiplies Portsmouth's influence
Unlocking tools like pillow tax and inclusionary zoning requires state action
cons: State legislative outcomes are uncertain and subject to partisan dynamics
Advocacy requires sustained effort over multiple legislative sessions
Some state mandates (like SB 284) create local implementation challenges

description: Partner with UNH planning program for research, data analysis, intern support.
metric: Research projects completed; interns placed; data products delivered
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan
pros: Access to research capacity, data analysis, and student interns at low cost
Academic research adds credibility to housing advocacy data
RHNA localization is a natural UNH project
cons: Academic timelines may not align with city policy windows
Student work requires supervision and quality control
Research findings may not always support preferred policy positions

description: Explore creation of a Portsmouth housing foundation to attract private capital.
metric: Foundation established; capital raised; projects funded
sources: Keene 1A; South Portland
pros: Attracts private philanthropic and corporate capital for housing
Tax-deductible contributions incentivize giving
Operates independently from city budget constraints
cons: Significant organizational startup costs and ongoing governance requirements
Fundraising capacity in a city of Portsmouth's size is uncertain
Potential duplication with existing Housing Trust Fund

description: Expand social services at PHA properties: mental health, job training, case management. RKG survey respondents specifically requested PHA onsite services.
metric: Services offered; residents served; outcomes (employment, stability)
sources: RKG 2022 (survey: onsite services request); HC Work Plan; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.7
pros: Wraparound services improve housing stability and reduce returns to homelessness
RKG survey respondents specifically requested expanded PHA services
Onsite delivery reduces transportation barriers for residents
cons: Ongoing operating costs for services beyond PHA's core housing mission
Staffing social services in a tight labor market is challenging
Service expansion requires coordination with existing community providers

action: Cross Roads House / homelessness system partnership
description: Formalize partnership with Cross Roads House and homeless services. Coordinate rapid rehousing with housing production.
metric: Partnership agreements; rapid rehousing placements; permanent housing exit rate
sources: RKG 2022 (homelessness data, Cross Roads House outcomes); HC Work Plan
pros: Formal partnership coordinates the continuum from shelter to permanent housing
Rapid rehousing is more cost-effective than extended shelter stays
39% adult exit rate to housing (per RKG) could improve with better coordination
cons: Rapid rehousing depends on available affordable units — a supply problem
Cross Roads House has its own capacity constraints
Homelessness services require sustained funding beyond initial partnership formation

description: Track and preserve Naturally Occurring Affordable Housing (NOAH) properties. Develop a NOAH registry, monitoring system, and preservation fund with acquisition and rehabilitation tools.
metric: NOAH properties tracked; preservation interventions; units preserved
sources: South Portland 1A; Keene 2A; Burien A3
notes: Incentives needed to get rental data from owners; financial assistance must be paired with legal agreements (deed restrictions/covenants) to lock in affordability.
pros: Prevents loss of affordable housing that took decades to develop organically
Preservation is often cheaper per unit than new construction
Deed restrictions ensure long-term affordability, not just current rents
cons: Tracking requires landlord participation — incentives are needed to get rental data
Financial assistance for renovations is an ongoing cost commitment
Property owners may resist deed restrictions as an encumbrance on their assets

description: Require registration of all rental units to track conditions, ownership, and pricing trends.
metric: Rental units registered; data reports produced; code compliance rate
sources: South Portland 2F; Burien D3; HC Work Plan
pros: Creates a comprehensive picture of rental housing stock, conditions, and pricing
Registration data supports evidence-based policy decisions
Can identify health and safety issues in rental units
cons: Registration fees and requirements may be passed through to tenants as higher rents
Enforcement requires dedicated code enforcement staff
Landlord opposition — may be seen as government overreach into private property

description: Adopt local tenant protections within NH legal authority: just cause notice periods, relocation assistance, source of income protections.
metric: Protections adopted; tenant complaints resolved; eviction notice compliance
sources: HAP Narrative; South Portland; Burien A5
notes: NH LEGAL VIABILITY: Under current NH landlord-tenant law (RSA 540), municipalities have limited authority for tenant regulation. Tools likely viable: extended notice periods, voluntary landlord incentive programs, relocation assistance funded through housing trust funds.
pros: Just-cause notice periods and relocation assistance reduce displacement hardship
Source of income protections expand access for voucher holders
Stabilizes communities by reducing involuntary moves
cons: NH's landlord-tenant legal framework limits municipal authority significantly
Landlords may argue protections reduce willingness to rent
Enforcement capacity must exist or protections are merely symbolic

description: Require notice, right of first refusal, or affordability requirements when rental properties convert to condos.
metric: Conversions with tenant protections; tenants retained or assisted
sources: HAP Narrative; South Portland
pros: Preserves rental housing supply — condo conversions remove rent-accessible units
Right of first refusal gives tenants an opportunity to purchase
Notice requirements reduce surprise displacement
cons: Restrictions may be challenged as limiting property owner rights under NH law
May discourage investment in rental properties if exit via condo conversion is limited
Scale of condo conversion activity in Portsmouth may not currently justify regulation

action: Lead paint and code compliance program
description: Proactive code enforcement targeting lead paint, fire safety, and habitability in older rental stock.
metric: Inspections completed; violations remediated; units brought to code
sources: Keene 2A; 2017 Listens
notes: NH Housing operates a lead abatement program that provides state funding for lead remediation — an existing resource to leverage.
pros: Directly improves health outcomes — lead exposure disproportionately harms children
NH Housing lead abatement program provides existing state funding to leverage
Proactive enforcement prevents deterioration into uninhabitable conditions
cons: Remediation costs can be significant — may cause some landlords to exit the rental market
Aggressive code enforcement may reduce affordable housing supply if landlords sell or convert
Inspection capacity requires dedicated staff

description: Establish metrics tracking displacement: demographic changes, out-migration, rental price changes by neighborhood.
metric: Annual displacement report published; neighborhoods monitored
sources: HAP Narrative; Burien A5; South Portland
pros: Data-driven tracking creates accountability for displacement prevention goals
Enables early intervention before displacement becomes severe
Demographic change data strengthens advocacy for preservation policies
cons: Monitoring alone doesn't prevent displacement — it identifies the problem
Data collection across multiple sources is complex and time-consuming
Defining and measuring 'displacement' is methodologically challenging

description: When rental properties sell, provide tenants or designated organizations right of first offer/refusal.
metric: Right-to-purchase notifications; tenant/nonprofit acquisitions
sources: HAP Narrative; South Portland
notes: May need state enabling legislation review
pros: Empowers tenants to preserve their own housing stability through ownership
Designated organizations can acquire and maintain affordability long-term
Prevents speculative purchases that lead to rent increases
cons: Tenants rarely have the financial capacity to exercise purchase rights
Right of first offer/refusal delays sales, potentially lowering property values
NH legal framework for such programs is untested — legal risk

description: Emergency grants/loans for homeowners facing foreclosure, major repairs, or tax delinquency.
metric: Homeowners assisted; home retention rate
sources: Burien A5; 2017 Listens
pros: Prevents foreclosure and displacement — far cheaper than rehousing
Stabilizes neighborhoods by keeping homes occupied and maintained
Targets crisis moments where a small amount of assistance has outsized impact
cons: Ongoing program funding required with no asset created
Eligibility determination is sensitive — risk of fraud or misuse
May delay inevitable outcomes for financially distressed homeowners

description: Track impact of STR conversions on long-term housing supply. Enforce registration.
metric: STR registrations; long-term units lost/recovered; enforcement actions
sources: South Portland 2F; HC Work Plan
pros: Tracking ensures the existing STR ban is actually effective
Data on enforcement actions supports continued policy defense
Low-cost activity relative to other housing preservation strategies
cons: Monitoring without enforcement is ineffective — requires staff capacity
Draws attention to STR policy that some want reopened
Limited impact if STR violations are minimal under current ban

description: Proactively engage owners of federally subsidized housing approaching subsidy expiration. RKG (2022) identified 489 units in three Portsmouth properties as potentially at risk of subsidy expiration by 2040. These figures have not been verified with PHA. First step is direct PHA outreach to verify current subsidy status.
target: Zero net loss of subsidized units — baseline verification required before specific targets are set
metric: Subsidized units preserved; units at risk identified; recapitalization deals completed
sources: RKG 2022 (NHPD data: 489 units at risk); South Portland 1A
notes: PROVISIONAL DATA: RKG 2022 unit counts (489 units: Osprey Landing 263 LIHTC, Wamesit Place 100 LIHTC, Keefe House 58 S8) are drawn from NHPD database and have not been verified with PHA.
pros: 489 federally subsidized units at risk is a significant share of Portsmouth's affordable stock
Proactive engagement gives city time to plan preservation strategies before expiration
Preservation costs far less per unit than building replacement affordable housing
cons: Property owners may prefer to exit subsidy programs for market-rate rents
City has limited leverage over federal subsidy decisions
Preservation financing is complex and competitive

description: Systematic condition assessment of pre-1960 housing stock (61% of owner-occupied, 46% of rental). Identify properties needing critical repairs, accessibility upgrades, weatherization.
metric: Properties assessed; conditions catalogued; rehab pipeline established
sources: RKG 2022 (housing stock age data); Keene 2A
pros: Systematic assessment identifies the most critical rehabilitation needs
Data supports targeted deployment of rehabilitation loans and grants
Prevents loss of aging housing stock through deterioration
cons: Property-by-property assessment is labor-intensive and costly
Property owners may resist inspections as intrusive
Assessment findings may reveal more need than available rehabilitation funding

description: Comprehensive strategy for senior housing: independent living, assisted living, memory care, aging-in-place support.
metric: Senior housing units by type; waiting list reduction; aging-in-place supports
sources: HC Work Plan (Loane); Keene 4C; 2016 Master Plan; RKG 2022; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.3
pros: Comprehensive strategy addresses all stages from independent living to memory care
Coordinated planning prevents gaps in the senior housing pipeline
Aging-in-place support reduces the most expensive care options
cons: Comprehensive strategy is planning-intensive — implementation may be slow
Market already provides some senior housing options — public role unclear for all segments
Assisted living and memory care are primarily private-sector products

description: Support transitional housing with wraparound services for people exiting homelessness, recovery, or institutional settings. Include partnerships with HAVEN (domestic violence services).
metric: Transitional units created; residents served; permanent housing transition rate
sources: Keene 1B; Burien A4; HC Work Plan; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.3
pros: Wraparound services dramatically improve outcomes for vulnerable populations
More cost-effective than cycling through emergency services and shelters
Addresses root causes of housing instability, not just symptoms
cons: Supportive housing is expensive to operate — requires sustained social service funding
Siting can face significant neighborhood opposition (NIMBYism)
Ongoing service provision requires partnerships with social service agencies

action: Assisted living and memory care expansion
description: Support assisted living and memory care affordable to moderate-income seniors.
metric: Assisted living beds added; affordability of new beds
sources: HC Work Plan (Loane); RKG 2022; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.3
pros: Moderate-income affordable options are virtually nonexistent — clear market gap
Frees up family housing when seniors move to appropriate settings
Addresses a growing need as Portsmouth's population ages
cons: Affordable assisted living is extremely difficult to finance — operating costs are high
Primarily a private-market product — city role is limited to incentives and zoning
Regulatory complexity in healthcare-adjacent housing

description: Assess unmet housing needs for people with physical and cognitive disabilities. Support universal design.
metric: Accessible units created; disability housing needs assessment completed
sources: HC Work Plan; RKG 2022 (survey: accessibility features); 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.3
pros: People with disabilities face severe housing barriers — documented unmet need
Universal design benefits all residents as they age
Aligns with Fair Housing Act obligations
cons: Accessible housing costs more to build than conventional units
Needs assessment requires engagement with disability communities, which takes time
Small population size makes dedicated programs difficult to sustain

description: Veteran-specific housing assistance. RKG identifies 1,366 veterans at median household income of $43,669 (~50% AMI). Coordinate with Harbor Care NH and VA.
metric: Veterans assisted; veteran housing placements; veteran cost burden rate
sources: RKG 2022 (veteran demographics; Harbor Care data); 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.3
pros: Veterans at $43,669 median income (~50% AMI) are significantly housing-burdened
Federal VA programs (VASH vouchers, SSVF) provide existing funding to leverage
Strong public and political support for veteran services
cons: Veteran-specific programs may duplicate existing general housing assistance
1,366 veterans is a relatively small target population for dedicated programs
VA program navigation is complex — requires specialized knowledge

description: Address housing for younger millennials (25-34) being priced out. RKG documents net out-migration of this cohort. Partnerships with GBCC, UNH; micro-units and shared housing models.
metric: Units targeting young professionals; age 25-34 retention; student housing beds
sources: RKG 2022 (migration patterns); Bill Bowen (GBCC at Pease); 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.3
pros: Addresses documented out-migration of 25-34 age cohort — critical for workforce
Micro-units and shared housing models can serve this population affordably
GBCC and UNH partnerships provide institutional anchors
cons: Young professionals may be a transient population with limited community investment
Micro-units and shared housing may not address the needs of those starting families
Market-rate micro-units may still be unaffordable in Portsmouth's price environment

description: Ongoing public education on affordability challenges. Social media, forums, local press.
metric: Engagement metrics; forum attendance; media coverage
sources: Keene 1D; Progress Portsmouth
pros: Public understanding is a prerequisite for political support for housing reform
Social media and local press are low-cost channels for reaching residents
Data-driven messaging makes abstract issues concrete
cons: Awareness campaigns don't directly produce housing
Messaging risks backlash if perceived as advocacy rather than information
Sustained campaigns require ongoing content production and management

description: Create/enhance city website with housing resources: programs, applications, ADU guidance, data dashboard.
metric: Portal launched; page visits; resource downloads
sources: Keene 4D; HAP Narrative
status_detail: City maintains Housing page with reports, data, committee info. Housing Related Reports & Data page is fairly comprehensive; could be enhanced
pros: Centralizes information that is currently scattered across city departments and websites
Existing Housing Related Reports page provides a foundation to build on
Self-service resource reduces staff time spent answering basic inquiries
cons: Website development and maintenance require staff or consultant resources
Static websites quickly become outdated without regular content updates
Digital-only resources don't reach residents without internet access

action: Annual housing report to City Council
description: Planning Department annual report on housing implementation, production vs. targets, affordability metrics.
target: Annual report published
metric: Report published on schedule; metrics tracked year-over-year
sources: Progress Tracker; Housing Policy 2016-01 §VI.6 Annual Reports
pros: Creates regular accountability mechanism for housing goals and production targets
Trend data over multiple years reveals whether policies are working
Public reporting builds credibility with residents and developers
cons: Annual report requires consistent data collection and staff time to produce
Reports risk becoming pro-forma exercises if Council doesn't act on findings
Production tracking depends on having clear targets to measure against

description: Educate employers on workforce housing challenges and available assistance programs.
metric: Employers contacted; surveys distributed; programs adopted
sources: Keene 1C; 2016 Master Plan (3.2.6)
pros: Employers are natural allies — housing affordability directly affects their workforce
Education is the prerequisite to employer participation in housing programs
Low-cost activity with potential for high-impact partnerships
cons: Education alone doesn't produce employer action — follow-up is essential
Employer attention is limited — housing competes with many business priorities
May raise expectations that employers will solve the problem

description: Accessible infographics explaining housing programs. Recruit volunteer designers. Distribute via website, social media, events.
metric: Materials created; distribution reach; program inquiry increases
sources: HC Work Plan (Migirditch)
pros: Visual communication makes complex housing data accessible to general public
Volunteer designers reduce cost while building community engagement
Shareable formats extend reach beyond city meetings and reports
cons: Infographics can oversimplify nuanced housing issues
Maintaining accuracy requires review as data changes
Design quality varies with volunteer capacity

description: Fair housing education for landlords, tenants, and real estate professionals. Monitor discrimination complaints.
metric: Training sessions held; complaints addressed; fair housing violations
sources: Keene 1D; HUD requirements; HC Work Plan
pros: Fair housing compliance is a legal obligation — education reduces violations
Source-of-income discrimination limits voucher utilization in tight markets
Educated landlords are less likely to engage in unintentional discrimination
cons: Education is necessary but not sufficient — enforcement matters more
NH legal framework for fair housing enforcement at the local level is limited
Landlords may view fair housing training as burdensome or adversarial

description: Meaningful resident engagement: early/ongoing input, representative voices, accessible forums, plain-language materials.
metric: Engagement events held; demographic diversity; input incorporated
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan; Burien C1
status_detail: Housing Blue Ribbon Committee reestablished March 2024 with working groups (Zoning, Parking/Transportation, Financial Tools); regular public meetings ongoing
pros: Representative engagement produces policies that reflect community values
Early involvement reduces opposition later in the process
Plain-language materials expand participation beyond regular meeting attendees
cons: Meaningful engagement is resource-intensive — poorly done engagement is worse than none
Engaged voices are often not representative of most-affected populations
Engagement fatigue — residents may disengage if they feel input isn't used

description: Continue and expand the Places to Live Dialogue community conversation model. Track the 11 dialogue goals.
metric: Dialogue sessions held; goals with measurable progress; new participants
sources: HC Work Plan (PtL Goals 1-11)
status_detail: Completed 2024; summary report published; findings fed into Housing Committee and Master Plan work
pros: Established format with documented outcomes and community trust
11 dialogue goals provide measurable framework for follow-up
Broad participation model reaches beyond typical public hearing attendees
cons: Dialogue outcomes are advisory — implementation depends on Council action
Continuing the same format may not reach new voices
Staff time to organize and facilitate is significant

description: Financial counseling addressing homeownership barriers from RKG survey: student debt (40%), car loans (34%), medical costs (38%). Pre-purchase counseling, credit repair, debt management.
metric: Counseling participants; credit score improvements; homebuyers assisted
sources: RKG 2022 (survey: financial barriers); Keene 1B; 2017 Listens; 2017 Master Plan Action 3.2.4
pros: Directly addresses documented barriers: student debt (40%), car loans (34%), medical costs (38%)
Pre-purchase counseling reduces mortgage default rates
Builds household financial stability that extends beyond housing
cons: Counseling addresses demand-side barriers but doesn't create affordable supply
Participation rates in financial counseling programs are often low
Programs require partnerships with certified HUD counseling agencies

action: Land use board HAP orientation program
description: Provide structured orientation for all land use board members — Planning Board, ZBA, HDC, TAC, and Conservation Commission — on the Housing Action Plan's goals, priorities, and their role in implementation.
target: All land use board members oriented within 6 months of HAP adoption; annual refresher sessions
metric: Orientation sessions held; board member attendance; integration of HAP goals into board decision-making
sources: Progress Portsmouth (2026)
notes: Board member familiarity with HAP priorities is directly relevant to implementation: land use boards make the decisions that determine whether zoning and permitting reforms produce actual housing.
pros: Board members make the land use decisions that determine whether HAP actions translate to housing
Orientation creates shared understanding across boards that currently operate independently
New appointee onboarding ensures continuity as board membership changes
cons: Board members serve voluntarily — mandatory training may deter participation
Orientation content must be carefully balanced to inform rather than prescribe outcomes
Annual refreshers require ongoing coordination and content development

action: Municipal infrastructure investment for housing areas
description: Proactively plan and fund water, sewer, street, utility improvements in areas designated for housing growth.
metric: CIP projects supporting housing areas; infrastructure capacity added
sources: Progress Tracker; Bill Bowen; 2017 Listens; Housing Policy 2016-01 §VI.5 Municipal Investment
pros: Proactive infrastructure investment removes a major barrier to housing development
Spreading costs across developments is more efficient than project-by-project upgrades
Demonstrates city commitment to housing growth areas
cons: Infrastructure investment requires significant capital expenditure from city budget
Benefits accrue to future development — timing of return on investment is uncertain
Prioritizing housing areas may delay infrastructure needs elsewhere

description: Develop municipal parking solutions separate from individual sites. Structured parking, developer contributions, shared facilities.
metric: Municipal parking spaces added; developer contributions collected
sources: Bill Bowen; HAP Narrative; Housing Policy 2016-01 §V.2 Parking; 2017 Master Plan Action 4.3.2
pros: Separating parking from individual sites reduces development costs and enables more housing
Shared facilities are more efficient than duplicated project-by-project parking
Developer parking contributions create a funding mechanism
cons: Structured parking is very expensive to build — $30K-$60K per space
Downtown parking is politically contentious with residents and businesses
Municipal parking facilities require ongoing operational management

description: Invest in transit, bike, and pedestrian infrastructure to reduce car dependence and support reduced parking requirements.
metric: Bike lane miles; transit ridership; mode share changes
sources: RKG 2022 (survey: transit concerns); HC Work Plan; South Portland 1C; Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026)
pros: Reducing car dependence supports reduced parking requirements for housing
Transit, bike, and pedestrian improvements benefit all residents
Aligns with climate and sustainability goals
cons: Significant public investment required for meaningful multimodal infrastructure
Portsmouth's geography and low density make transit service challenging
Behavioral change from car dependence is slow regardless of infrastructure

action: School capacity assessment for housing areas
description: Document existing school capacity data by attendance zone and make it publicly accessible. Portsmouth has significant existing capacity, particularly in the downtown area (Little Harbor attendance zone).
metric: School capacity assessment completed; housing plans aligned
sources: HC Work Plan (Corsetti); RKG 2022 (enrollment projections); Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026)
notes: No additional study needed; capacity exists. Reframe toward documenting existing capacity as an advocacy tool.
pros: Publicly documented capacity data enables informed discussion of housing impacts on schools.
School Board coordination ensures housing planning aligns with enrollment trends.
Surplus school properties are often well-located in neighborhoods.
cons: Capacity documentation alone doesn't address school quality concerns
Enrollment projections are inherently uncertain — capacity claims may be challenged
School capacity varies by grade level and program — aggregate numbers can be misleading

description: Determine growth capacity for specific critical systems: sewer treatment plant capacity, water supply, fire department response coverage, stormwater management capacity, and floodplain buildable acreage constraints. Map growth areas where infrastructure can support additional housing without major capital investment.
metric: Capacity analysis completed; growth areas identified and mapped
sources: HC Work Plan (Corsetti; PtL Goal 4)
pros: Comprehensive capacity data prevents infrastructure being used as a false excuse to block housing
Mapping growth areas helps target housing incentives and zoning changes
Data supports capital improvement planning
cons: Analysis can be used to argue against development as easily as for it
Comprehensive infrastructure studies are expensive and time-consuming
Capacity findings become outdated as conditions change

description: Establish specific annual and 5-year production targets for total units and by affordability level. Byron Matto's comprehensive GIS analysis provides the parcel-level foundation for this inventory.
metric: Units produced vs. targets annually; affordable unit share
sources: HAP Narrative; Keene 3C; Burien B2
pros: Specific targets create accountability and measurable progress
Targets by affordability level ensure production serves a range of incomes
Annual reporting against targets makes housing a regular policy conversation
cons: Targets without implementation tools are aspirational, not actionable
City has limited direct control over private-market housing production
Setting targets too high invites criticism; too low normalizes inadequate production

description: Maintain GIS-based inventory of developable parcels, underutilized sites, and housing pipeline projects.
metric: GIS inventory completed and maintained; parcels in active pipeline
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan
pros: Visual mapping of developable parcels makes opportunities concrete for developers
Pipeline tracking shows what's in progress and where gaps remain
GIS analysis can quantify ADU and conversion potential
cons: GIS maintenance requires ongoing staff time and data updates
Parcel-level analysis may identify sites with political or neighborhood sensitivities
Inventory shows potential, not probability — landowners may not be interested in developing

description: Regular updates (every 2-3 years) to housing needs assessment and gap analysis.
metric: Gap analysis updated; data currency; identified gaps addressed
sources: Keene 3C; South Portland; Burien B2
status_detail: RKG 2022 study aging; HOP 2.0 audit partially updates picture; Master Plan (2025–2026) will include housing needs analysis but no standalone updated gap analysis commissioned
pros: RKG 2022 study is aging — market conditions have shifted significantly
Updated data prevents advocacy from relying on outdated numbers
Master Plan housing needs analysis may partially serve this function
cons: Comprehensive gap analyses cost $100K+ and take months to complete
Risk of analysis paralysis — waiting for perfect data delays action on known needs
Frequent updates may show marginal changes that don't justify the cost

description: Public-facing dashboard tracking housing metrics: production, affordability, permits, vacancy, cost burden.
metric: Dashboard launched; data updated quarterly; public usage metrics
sources: HAP Narrative; HC Work Plan; Burien B2
pros: Real-time public data builds transparency and supports evidence-based advocacy
Dashboard automates what would otherwise be manual data compilation
Consistent metrics enable year-over-year trend tracking
cons: Data infrastructure and dashboard development require upfront investment
Some housing data (vacancy, rents) is difficult to collect reliably at the municipal level
Dashboards require ongoing maintenance or become misleading

description: Production targets calibrated by AMI band. RKG projects 52% of demand from households at ≤80% AMI, 85% from 1-2 person households. Total demand: 3,124 units by 2030.
target: 52% of new units affordable at ≤80% AMI
metric: Production by AMI band vs. targets; unit size distribution; owner vs. renter mix
sources: RKG 2022 (demand projections by AMI and household size)
notes: The ≤50% AMI segment represents the most severe affordability gap in Portsmouth.
pros: AMI calibration ensures production targets match the income distribution of demand
RKG data (52% of demand at ≤80% AMI) provides the baseline for calibration
Prevents a scenario where 'housing production' means only luxury units
cons: Production at lower AMI levels requires subsidy — targets may exceed available funding
AMI bands are somewhat arbitrary — household circumstances vary within each band
Market-rate development doesn't respond to AMI targets without incentives or requirements

description: Establish healthy vacancy target (4-6%) and monitor. RKG documents 1.86% combined vacancy — well below functional market threshold.
target: Achieve 4% combined vacancy rate
metric: Quarterly vacancy rate tracking; owner vs. renter vacancy; months of supply
sources: RKG 2022 (vacancy analysis)
notes: Achieving a functional 4-6% vacancy rate likely requires on the order of 800-1,200+ net new units over the planning horizon.
pros: 1.86% vacancy is well below the 4-6% functional market threshold — data makes this visible
Tracking vacancy over time shows whether policies are improving market function
Clear target (4-6%) provides a benchmark for progress
cons: Municipal-level vacancy data is notoriously unreliable — collection methods matter
Vacancy rate alone doesn't distinguish between affordable and luxury vacancies
Achieving 4-6% vacancy requires significant net new production in a built-out city

description: Conduct primary research with Portsmouth's most housing-insecure residents: cost-burdened renters, seniors aging in place, people with disabilities, individuals experiencing or at risk of homelessness.
metric: Survey completed; sample size and demographic coverage; findings published and integrated into HAP priorities
sources: Advocate feedback (2026); RKG 2022 (survey limitations: PHA-focused); Rockingham County RHNA
notes: Coordinate with Cross Roads House, ServiceLink, local churches, and PHA for participant access
pros: Primary research fills a critical gap — existing data doesn't capture Portsmouth-specific conditions
Directly engages the people most affected by housing challenges
Findings strengthen advocacy by putting faces and stories behind statistics
cons: Surveying vulnerable populations requires sensitivity, trust-building, and ethical protocols
Sample sizes for specific subgroups may be too small for statistically robust findings
Research takes time — results may arrive after policy windows close

description: Systematic survey of Portsmouth businesses and organizations quantifying recruitment and retention challenges attributable to housing costs.
metric: Employers surveyed; positions affected quantified; economic impact estimated; results published
sources: Advocate feedback (2026); Keene 1C (employer partnerships); Bill Bowen (Service Credit Union, Novocure, Lonza)
notes: Complements employer roundtable — this is the data-gathering step that precedes partnership formation
pros: Quantifies the economic cost of housing unaffordability — a powerful argument for action
Employer data reframes housing as a business competitiveness issue
Precursor to employer roundtable — builds the evidence base first
cons: Survey response rates from businesses are typically low
Employers may be reluctant to share salary and recruitment data
Results may confirm what's already known without changing the political calculus

description: Adapt Rockingham County Regional Housing Needs Assessment findings to Portsmouth-specific context. Fills analytical gap between RKG's PHA-focused 2022 study and the RHNA's regional scope.
metric: Localized analysis completed; Portsmouth-specific demand figures published; findings integrated into production targets
sources: Advocate feedback (2026); Rockingham County RHNA; RKG 2022
notes: May be achievable through UNH partnership or consultant engagement at relatively low cost
pros: Bridges the gap between county-level RHNA data and Portsmouth-specific needs
UNH partnership provides low-cost research capacity
Calibrates regional housing needs to local demographics and market conditions
cons: Methodology for localizing county data involves assumptions that can be challenged
May duplicate portions of the Master Plan housing needs analysis
Academic partnerships may produce findings on slower timelines than advocacy needs

description: Annual monitoring of housing utilization and ownership patterns — including non-primary residence share and ownership concentration trends — with policy review triggered if significant shifts affect effective housing supply.
sources: Chellman/Duffy per discussion of Vancouver syndrome
notes: SEQUENCING NOTE: This item should be initiated immediately — it is not in the standard triage queue. Portsmouth's studio median rent of $2,300/month is consistent with non-primary-residence ownership patterns.
status_detail: Not yet initiated. Elevated to high-urgency pre-triage item.
pros: Provides early warning of shifts in housing utilization and ownership patterns
Supports data-driven decision-making rather than reactive policy debates
Aligns with production targets by ensuring new units are functioning as intended
cons: Data collection may be imperfect or require cross-department coordination
Monitoring does not by itself increase housing supply
Some ownership or utilization data may be difficult to access or verify

action: Development feasibility and pro forma analysis (2026 market conditions)
description: Commission or conduct updated development feasibility analysis reflecting 2026 market conditions. Analysis should include pro forma modeling for representative housing types under current cost and financing conditions, break-even rent and sale price estimates, and sensitivity analysis showing how feasibility changes under different incentive scenarios.
target: Updated feasibility analysis completed within 6 months of HAP adoption. Refreshed every 2 years.
metric: Break-even rent estimates by housing type; gap between break-even rents and target AMI affordability levels; number of incentive scenarios modeled
sources: Reviewer feedback (2026); Progress Portsmouth (2026); Byron Matto GIS analysis (Jan 2026)
notes: Without current feasibility data, incentives may be miscalibrated — too small to change developer behavior, or too generous relative to actual need.
pros: Calibrates incentives to actual development economics rather than assumptions from 2022
Break-even rent analysis shows exactly where the affordability gap is and how large the subsidy needs to be
Sensitivity analysis lets policymakers see which incentive levers have the most impact per dollar
cons: Pro forma analysis requires developer input and market data that may be proprietary or contested
Results become outdated quickly in volatile cost environments — requires regular updating
Analysis may show that some housing types are simply infeasible without deep subsidy

action: Buildout capacity modeling (current vs. reformed zoning)
description: Model the theoretical and realistic housing production capacity under current zoning versus proposed zoning reforms. Analysis should show current by-right theoretical capacity, realistic capacity, reformed capacity, and the gap between reformed capacity and the RKG demand projection of 3,124 units.
target: Initial buildout model completed within 6 months of HAP adoption. Updated as zoning reforms are adopted.
metric: Theoretical vs. realistic unit capacity under current and reformed zoning; gap to 3,124-unit demand projection
sources: Reviewer feedback (2026); Progress Portsmouth (2026)
notes: This is the analytical bridge between zoning reform proposals and production targets.
pros: Connects zoning reform proposals directly to unit production estimates — makes the case for reform concrete.
Visual gap analysis makes housing capacity and demand data accessible for public and Council review.
Realistic capacity adjustments prevent overpromising what zoning changes alone can deliver.
cons: Modeling requires assumptions about owner willingness and market absorption that are inherently uncertain
Theoretical capacity numbers can be misleadingly large
Parcel-level analysis may identify politically sensitive sites, creating opposition before reforms are even proposed

action: Climate resiliency requirements for new housing
description: Integrate climate change into zoning and building requirements. Prioritize environmental sustainability.
metric: Climate provisions adopted; green-certified housing units
sources: HC Work Plan (Loane; PtL Goal 4); 2017 Master Plan Actions 5.1.1, 5.4.7
pros: Climate integration protects housing investment from flood, heat, and storm damage
Proactive standards avoid costly retrofits and insurance increases later
Aligns housing policy with city climate action goals

Regulatory burden may discourage housing production if requirements are excessive

description: Outreach on climate-conscious retrofitting. Collaborate on sustainable development approaches.
metric: Retrofit participants; energy efficiency improvements; carbon reduction
sources: HC Work Plan (Loane; Snyder); 2017 Master Plan Action 5.1.1
pros: Energy efficiency reduces utility costs for occupants — an affordability benefit
Climate-conscious retrofits extend the useful life of aging housing stock
Federal incentives (IRA, Weatherization Assistance) provide funding leverage
cons: Retrofit costs can be significant, especially for older buildings
Outreach and uptake are typically slow without aggressive promotion
Some retrofits may trigger other code upgrades, increasing total project cost

description: Ensure new housing accounts for flood risk and sea level rise. Update flood maps and building standards.
metric: Housing in updated flood zones; flood-resilient standards adopted
sources: HC Work Plan (PtL Goal 4); Portsmouth Climate Action Plan; 2017 Master Plan Actions 5.1.3, 5.5.1
pros: Portsmouth's coastal location makes flood risk a real and growing concern
Updated flood maps protect future housing from known hazards
Building standards that account for sea level rise are cost-effective when applied at construction
cons: Updated flood maps may constrain development in areas currently zoned for housing
Higher flood resilience standards increase construction costs
Climate adaptation requirements add regulatory complexity for developers

action: HAP formal adoption and governance framework
description: Establish a formal adoption mechanism for the Housing Action Plan as a binding policy document. This includes: City Council resolution or ordinance formally adopting the HAP; designation of a lead department and responsible staff positions; establishment of a HAP Implementation Committee; and integration of HAP priorities into the city's annual goal-setting and strategic planning processes.
target: Council adoption within 6 months of HAP completion. Implementation Committee established within 90 days of adoption.
metric: Formal adoption vote (yes/no); Implementation Committee formed and meeting (yes/no); HAP referenced in annual city goals (yes/no).
notes: Formal adoption and named implementation ownership provide the governance foundation for the remaining action items.
sources: Reviewer feedback (2026); Progress Portsmouth (2026)
pros: Transforms the HAP from advisory to binding — creates legal and political accountability
Named departmental ownership ensures someone is responsible for driving implementation
Formal adoption gives the HAP standing in budget deliberations and land use decisions
cons: Council may resist binding adoption if it constrains future discretion on individual projects
Formal governance structure adds administrative overhead and meeting obligations
Political risk if Council adopts the HAP but then visibly fails to implement it

description: Establish a quarterly implementation dashboard and annual progress report to City Council tracking HAP action item status, housing production against targets, and resource deployment.
target: Quarterly dashboard operational within 6 months of HAP adoption. First annual report within 12 months.
metric: Dashboard published quarterly (yes/no); annual report presented to Council (yes/no); percentage of HAP action items advanced per year.
notes: Regular reporting on HAP implementation creates an ongoing record of progress and supports informed decision-making by the Housing Committee, City Council, and the public.
sources: Reviewer feedback (2026); Progress Portsmouth (2026)
pros: Creates regular, visible accountability that prevents the HAP from being shelved after adoption
Public dashboard builds transparency and resident trust in the housing reform process
Quarterly cadence keeps housing on the Council agenda
cons: Reporting requires staff time to compile data — adds to already-stretched capacity
Public dashboards showing slow progress could be used to criticize the city rather than motivate action
Risk of measuring activity (items advanced) rather than impact (units produced, affordability achieved)

description: Require that the annual city budget process explicitly address HAP implementation funding. This includes: a dedicated HAP line item or program budget; a requirement that the City Manager's proposed budget identify which HAP actions are funded, partially funded, or unfunded; and a mechanism for the HAP Implementation Committee to submit budget recommendations.
target: Budget alignment process integrated into FY2028 budget cycle.
metric: HAP funding identified in annual budget (yes/no); percentage of HAP actions with dedicated funding; total annual HAP-related appropriation.
notes: This item ensures HAP implementation is reflected in the annual budget process and that associated costs are not absorbed as an unfunded load on existing staff.
sources: Reviewer feedback (2026); Progress Portsmouth (2026)
pros: Forces the city to put resources behind HAP commitments rather than treating it as an unfunded mandate
Annual budget alignment creates a natural prioritization and sequencing mechanism
Dedicated funding line item makes HAP spending visible and accountable to taxpayers
cons: Competes with other budget priorities in a fiscally constrained environment
Dedicated housing budget may be cut in lean years, undermining implementation continuity
Budget requirement may make Council less willing to adopt the HAP in the first place

action: Forensic review of land use board decision patterns for housing applications (2021–2025)
description: Commission a structured review of decisions by the Zoning Board of Adjustment, Historic District Commission, and Planning Board on housing applications over the prior four years to identify patterns in how each body exercises discretionary authority. The review examines three dimensions: (1) legal fidelity — whether denials were compelled by ordinance language or represented discretionary interpretations where approval was legally defensible under a fair reading of applicable standards; (2) Master Plan alignment — whether decisions reflect or contradict the housing production goals established in the adopted Master Plan, which the city's own zoning authority is legally grounded in; and (3) interpretive posture — whether boards apply the principle, established in Richmond Co. v. City of Concord (149 N.H. 312, 2003) and reinforced in Appeal of Town of Windham (2022), that the land use process is not adversarial and that boards have a constitutional obligation to work with applicants rather than treat ambiguity as grounds for denial. RSA 676:3,I (effective August 2022) requires specific written findings of fact to support any disapproval; the review will assess whether Portsmouth denials meet that standard. Four decisions across the review period have been identified where the record supports a credible legal defense of approval and will serve as anchor cases for the methodology.
target: Review completed and findings presented to the Housing Committee within 12 months of Housing Action Plan adoption. Findings incorporated into the zoning modernization consultant scope prior to RFP issuance.
metric: Review completed (yes/no); number of applications identified as discretionary denials by board; ordinance provisions flagged for excessive or vague discretionary language; Master Plan alignment gaps identified by board; written findings adequacy assessed under RSA 676:3,I; training or appointment recommendations issued by board (yes/no).
notes: The review should examine whether board decisions during the review period reflect the interpretive standards established in RSA 672:1, the written findings requirement of RSA 676:3,I (amended 2022), and the duty-to-assist doctrine established in NH case law. It should explicitly separate legal questions from policy questions and should not presuppose findings. Its purpose is to establish a documented, credible evidence base for two downstream actions: targeted ordinance revisions through zoning modernization, and evaluation of whether board appointment criteria and training adequately reflect applicable legal and constitutional standards.
sources: Richmond Co. v. City of Concord, 149 N.H. 312 (2003); Appeal of Town of Windham, NH Supreme Court No. 2021-0473 (2022); RSA 672:1, III-e; RSA 676:3,I (amended 2022); Dartmouth v. Hanover (2018); Stephen C. Buckley, NHMA Legal Services Counsel, Planning Board Basics (2024 Spring Planning and Zoning Conference)
pros: Establishes a credible, documented evidence base that distinguishes ordinance problems from board behavior problems — critical for targeting zoning modernization correctly
Frames the issue in legal and constitutional terms rather than political ones, reducing the risk of dismissal as advocacy
Directly feeds the zoning modernization consultant scope, ensuring the HAP drives that process rather than the reverse
RSA 676:3,I written findings requirement gives the review a concrete, objective standard against which to assess denials
cons: Politically sensitive — any finding that boards have denied legally defensible applications will be read as criticism of named volunteer members
Market-rate housing lacks the explicit statutory protection that workforce housing has under RSA 672:1,III-e, weakening the legal argument for applicant-favorable interpretation
Requires access to application records, minutes, and written decisions across three boards over four years — staff capacity and records quality may be limiting factors
Without a legal opinion accompanying the review, findings about 'legally defensible' approvals may be disputed

description: Establish a process for Housing Committee review of incoming GNOD land transfer offers (Section 10.686.30), including suitability assessment, Phase I coordination, and connection to nonprofit developer RFP process. Track accepted parcels and development rights credits (Section 10.686.40).
target: Process established within 6 months of first GNOD land transfer offer received
metric: Number of transfer offers received; parcels accepted; parcels connected to RFP or nonprofit developer pipeline; development rights credits tracked
sources: Portsmouth Zoning Ordinance Section 10.686.30 and 10.686.40 (as amended Feb 17, 2026)
notes: Land transfer decisions require City Council acceptance — Planning Board provides advisory comments only. City Council designates procurement, development form, and disposition for each accepted parcel. Section 10.686.40 development rights banking applies when transfers exceed the minimum (1 contiguous acre per 20 acres of GNOD development); credits carry forward to future GNOD projects by the same developer. Coordinating transfer acceptance decisions with RFP readiness (Phase I clearance completed, constraints site plan reviewed) reduces lag between acquisition and developer selection. Cross-references: Z-1, PL-1, PL-2, PL-4.
status_detail: No GNOD land transfer offers received to date. No administrative process established.
pros: Creates a structured pathway for private land to enter the affordable housing pipeline at no city acquisition cost
Development rights banking (Section 10.686.40) enables strategic parcel accumulation across multiple GNOD projects over time
City Council retains full discretion over acceptance — no obligation to accept unsuitable parcels
cons: Entirely dependent on GNOD development activity — no transfers occur without active GNOD projects
Transferred parcels may be irregular in size or location, limiting nonprofit developer interest
Administrative process adds staff capacity requirements during a period when Planning Dept is already stretched

action: PHA scattered-site project-based voucher program for private small multifamily
description: Explore a Housing Assistance Payments (HAP) contract program in which Portsmouth Housing Authority attaches project-based vouchers to income-restricted units in privately-owned small multifamily properties (2–20 units). Landlords receive market-equivalent revenue; tenants pay income-appropriate rents; the city gains de facto affordable units without new construction. Frame as a preservation program targeting naturally occurring affordable housing at risk of market-rate conversion.
target: Preliminary feasibility conversation with PHA completed within 6 months; program design developed within 12 months if PHA willing
metric: Number of PBV units placed in private small multifamily; number of landlord participants; units preserved at workforce rent levels
sources: HUD Housing Choice Voucher Program — Project-Based Voucher regulations (24 CFR Part 983); field-tested small developer input, spring 2026
notes: Federal law permits PHA to attach project-based vouchers to privately-owned scattered-site properties under 24 CFR Part 983. HUD site selection criteria and administrative requirements apply. Key conditions: (1) PHA must designate this as a strategic priority — not standard practice for small scattered-site properties; (2) HAP contract terms (typically 10–20 years) give lenders a revenue stream to underwrite against, improving landlord acquisition financing; (3) landlord must commit to income restriction for contract duration; (4) PHA administrative capacity for managing multiple small HAP contracts is a real constraint. A scattered-site PBV program could be co-designed with the city using HUD preservation criteria — HUD looks favorably on preservation framing. Cross-references: PC-8 (PHA coordination), I-14 (CDBG rehab), I-7 (tax credits).
status_detail: No conversation with PHA initiated. No HUD feasibility assessment completed.
pros: Solves the revenue-side cash flow problem for small landlords willing to income-restrict — more durable than a one-time grant
HAP contract gives lenders a predictable revenue stream to underwrite against, potentially improving acquisition financing
Preserves existing affordable stock without new construction cost or timeline
HUD preservation criteria may support program design and funding eligibility
cons: Requires PHA as willing partner — administrative capacity and strategic alignment with Craig Welch are preconditions
HUD site selection criteria for scattered-site properties may be difficult to meet for some small multifamily
PHA voucher pool is heavily committed — adding new HAP contracts competes with existing obligations
Program design and HUD approval process is complex and time-intensive
